entry.title: Considerations when mapping organizational data from Employee Central to SmartRecruiters
entry.content:
In this article we will raise a few points to consider when you map foundational data from SuccessFactors Employee Central to SmartRecruiters. 

Below is a standard foundation structure in Employee Central, all the objects are linked to each other and there is a custom level between division and department.  This is a fairly common set up.  

The job structure has two levels on top of the job code, again a common structure. 

SmartRecruiters has three standard fields, Country, Brand and Department.  You can not map data to the standard field and we would advise that you use these for recruitment and not to repurpose them with EC data.  If you are considering mapping the Department Foundational Object to an organizational field in SmartRecruiters then you will need to rename the field in SmartRecruiters as the Department label is already used.  Finally SmartRecruiters 1:1 parent child relationships and there are no multi select fields just like Employee Central.

SmartRecruiters has 22 organizational field limits.  A common misconception is to think that all the foundation objects in Employee Central have to map to these 22 fields and thus customers are limited.  This is an incorrect assumption. 

Rather than thinking as them as organizational data fields like we do in Employee Central, think of them as fields that allow you to ‘organize’ logic in SmartRecruiters.  For example, if you would like Legal Entity to drive logic in SmartRecruiters then it needs to be an organizational field.  However, if Division is not needed for logic then it can be a job field and not an organizational field.  You can split out different objects to org and job fields dependent on your business needs. Job and org fields can be parents.

Furthermore, you must think outside of your Employee Central Foundational Structure.  In Employee Central we have field called ‘Employee Class’ and this is typically on the position, and it can be a picklist or an object.  If you want Employee Class value to drive logic in SmartRecruiters then it needs to be an organization field not a job field. 

As a result you could end up something like this.

entry.title: Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings – A Game Changer in SAP SuccessFactors Time Management
entry.content:
Introduction:

One of my clients had a real business pain point where employees who work on a annual leave or any leave for that matter was unable to record the overtime in the Timesheet. It was due to the time collision that was restricting them to apply for the attendance which is Overtime. They had to manually keep a track in the excel sheet , compute and pay them Overtime.

How Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings came to the rescue!

Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings was introduced as part of the 1H 2026 Release which was one of the much awaited and highlighted features.To make the section visible , make sure the TLMTriggerTasksJob is scheduled in the System and is run. You can monitor the job from the Scheduled Job Manager

However if the job is not scheduled , it has to scheduled from the provisioning.

Next up is RBP, RBP needs to be provided to make the section visible .

 

Configurations to handle Attendance and Absence Groupings:

Step 1 : Create Time Collision Groupings 

Manage Data -> Create New -> Time Collision Grouping -> Category : Attendance

Step 2 : Create Time Collision Groupings 

Manage Data -> Create New -> Time Collision Grouping -> Category : Absence

Step 3 : Assigning the Groupings to the Time Type

Manage data -> Search -> Time Type -> Assign the Grouping to the Collision Grouping field

Step 4 : Create Time Management Collision Resolution 

Manage Data -> Create New -> Time  Management Collision Resolution 

Make sure to add the incoming and Existing Grouping are added vice versa.Decide on whether to show / hide the collision resolution message.I will advice it to hide so that the user doenst get confused mistaking it for error.

Collision Resolution Action - Allow Concurrent :

Will allow incoming entries to co-exist during the same timings with the existing grouping

Collision Resolution Action - Allow outside Absence Hours :

Will allow incoming entries to exist only outside the absence hours with the existing grouping

Business Benefits

Using Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings offers several advantages:

  • Eliminates manual overtime tracking in spreadsheets.
  • Reduces payroll processing effort and manual calculations.
  • Provides greater flexibility for organizations with unique attendance policies.
  • Improves data accuracy by allowing valid attendance and absence records to coexist.
  • Enhances the employee and administrator experience by reducing unnecessary collision errors.

    Conclusion :

    Organizations that previously relied on manual workarounds for scenarios such as overtime during leave can now configure the system to support these business requirements directly. With the right collision grouping and resolution settings, administrators gain greater control over how attendance and absence records interact, reducing administrative effort while ensuring accurate payroll processing.

entry.title: Ongoing Lessons Learnt integrating SmartRecruiters to SuccessFactors Onboarding
entry.content:
Over the past couple of months, I have been involved with a few projects implementing SmartRecruiters into an existing SuccessFactors landscape.  In this article I will summarize the current challenges and opportunities’ we are seeing.

As to my background with SAP SuccessFactors, I joined in 2010 to implement the current SuccessFactors Recruitment before concentrating on Employee Central and Onboarding 2.0.  I am not certified to implement SmartRecruiters, however I did do the first ever beta testing of SmartRecruiters in 2009 when it was owned by Mr Ted Talentlink!

So, what springs to mind?

Glossary/terms of reference

In SmartRecruiters things are called different things and this for experienced SuccessFactors people can cause confusion.

The Job Requisition does not exist, it’s called a ‘job’.  Which can be confusing as we refer to the job as the job code object.  But the job code and position are both attributes of the job in Smart Recruiters.  Whereas in Employee Central jobcodes are attributes of the position.

You have job and organization fields on the job.  As I wrote in this article about organization fields in SmartRecruiters they are not to be confused with the organizational structure in Employee Central.

There isn’t a specific rules engine in SmartRecruiters like we have in SuccessFactors to update fields, but there are automation capabilities that can be added to the Hiring Processes called Workflows. With Workflow Rules in SmartRecruiters, organizations can standardize their recruiting activities by triggering automated actions at different statuses of the hiring process. These include automatically sending notifications to Candidates and Hiring Team members, assigning tasks such as preparing offers or coordinating interviews, reminding recruiters to clean up their pipeline after a number of days, setting SLAs, and advancing candidates based on conditions.

 Master Data

When Employee Central first came on the scene, if you had conversations with an SAP onPrem expert, they viewed the organization structures in Employee Central as not being compatible with complex ERP system and lacking maturity.  In some cases, this was the cases but often the data was structured is differently.  10 years on I feel these conversations are happening again, this time between SmartRecruiters and Employee Central.  For example, organizational fields in SmartRecruiters only support 1:1 relationship.  It doesn’t mean it’s a blocker you need to focus on what you need to power your recruitment processes rather than trying to replicate the organizational structure in SmartRecruiters.  Remember the position is still leading. 

Data objects in SuccessFactors have a label which is translatable to different languages, but they share the same external code.  This is different in SmartRecruiters, it is all done by labels and not an external code, and each language translation has its own label.

Integrations

The integrations are constantly evolving; there has been numerous updates on the user and position to job integrations in the last few weeks.

Position to Job

One thing that is currently different with SmartRecruiters is any data you want to send from the position to SmartRecruiters needs to be on the position.  In SuccessFactors Recruitment you could reference data in ‘look up’ tables or associated data object on the position.  This is currently not possible.

SmartRecruiters back to Onboarding

This is the area that is causing the most headaches (for me) as I try to understand how the full processes will all blend together.  I wrote a long article on how all the processes work with SuccessFactors Recruitment/Onboarding and Employee Central work for new hires, internal hires, concurrent employments and global assignments.  As you will see we have lots of options.

However, with SmartRecruiters as of June 28 we only support new hires into onboarding, you can’t send the data directly to Employee Central via MPH.  Furthermore, not all data elements can be mapped specifically, only Personal Information, Job Information, Employment Information, and Phone Information are supported. All other portlets, custom MDFs and pay components are not supported.

If you have some countries or employee types that do not use onboarding, then you can’t choose to send these directly to MPH like you can with SuccessFactors Recruitment they all need to go via onboarding.  Therefore, for those who you want to go to MPH you will need to send them to onboarding with an empty process variant.  You may want to build a custom field in SmartRecruiters and map this to employment details and use this field in your process variant rule.

I think it is more accurate to say the integration is from SmartRecruiters to Onboarding.

If you do not have SuccessFactors Onboarding then the candidate will land in MPH.

As of July 15th please see this article for more information regarding Internal hires..

These integrations are actively being worked on over the next few months.  As they change, I will update this blog

Finally, when you send data back to SuccessFactors you will need to ensure all the rules work correctly in Onboarding and employee Central.  I wrote this article about things to consider, and it is still extremely relevant.

 Rehire checks 

When you initiate onboarding from SmartRecruiters the first rehire check is not initiated, please see this article for more details

How would I proceed

  1. Don’t get hung up about the integrations, they are evolving.  Start you workshops for SmartRecruiters.
  2. Take time to evaluate what you send from the position to SmartRecruiters, don’t just copy what you did with SuccessFactors Recruitment. 
  3. Review your position management processes specifically what data you store on the position, the approval process and how this will work with job approval process in SmartRecruiters.  Now maybe a good time to review these?
  4. If you are using a niche SmartRecruiters partner for your implementation, ensure they have architect assigned who has a good understanding of SuccessFactors Onboarding, Employee Central and the data models.

I will continue to update this article over the next few months.

entry.title: How We Built an Audit and Compliance Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM Using SAP BTP and Joule
entry.content:
Introduction
In most organizations using SAP SuccessFactors HCM, configuration changes and employee data updates happen every day. New fields are added, business rules are modified, permissions are updated, and employee records are continuously maintained across multiple system environments.

During our work with SAP SuccessFactors implementations, we frequently observed how difficult it was for administrators and compliance teams to maintain visibility into these changes. Organizations often operate multiple environments such as Development, Test, and Production, and even small configuration differences between them can lead to unexpected issues, failed deployments, or inconsistent business processes.

At the same time, tracking employee data changes is not always straightforward. Understanding who modified a record, when the change occurred, what values were updated, or whether a record was deleted often requires navigating multiple modules and reports. Preparing for audits or compliance reviews can quickly become a time-consuming and manual process.

We also noticed that maintaining configuration documentation was another major challenge. As systems evolve, documentation often becomes outdated, making it difficult for teams to validate configurations, troubleshoot issues, or ensure compliance with organizational standards.

The real challenge was not simply collecting audit information—it was providing a centralized and intelligent way to compare configurations, track changes, monitor compliance activities, and generate accurate documentation across the entire SAP SuccessFactors landscape.

This realization led us to build an Audit and Compliance Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM using SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and SAP Joule. The solution was designed to help organizations improve governance, strengthen compliance, reduce manual effort, and gain complete visibility into both configuration and employee data changes.

Our Approach
To address these challenges, we built an Audit and Compliance Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and integrated it with SAP Joule to provide a centralized and intelligent auditing experience.

While designing this solution, one of our primary goals was to simplify how organizations monitor, validate, and govern their SAP SuccessFactors landscape. Instead of relying on multiple reports, manual comparisons, and fragmented audit information, we wanted to bring everything together into a single platform that provides complete visibility into system and employee-related changes.

What mattered most during development was usability and transparency. Administrators should be able to quickly identify configuration differences between environments, track employee data modifications, review permission changes, monitor recruitment activities, and generate documentation without spending hours collecting information from different sources.

We also wanted to reduce the dependency on manual audits and documentation efforts. By automating metadata comparison, workbook generation, and change tracking, the solution enables organizations to focus on analysis and decision-making rather than data collection.

With this approach, organizations can confidently maintain consistency across environments, strengthen compliance processes, improve governance, and gain complete visibility into configuration and employee data changes while significantly reducing operational effort.

Enhancing the Experience with SAP Joule

While the Audit and Compliance Solution provides comprehensive auditing capabilities, integrating it with Joule makes accessing audit information faster and more intuitive.

Instead of navigating multiple screens or manually searching through audit reports, administrators can interact with the solution using natural language. Joule interprets user requests and helps retrieve relevant audit information, compare configurations, and initiate documentation generation through a conversational interface.

Some examples include:

  • Compare metadata between Development, Test, and Production environments.
  • Retrieve employee data audit history.
  • Review Role-Based Permission (RBP) changes.
  • View deleted employee records.
  • Generate configuration or business rule workbooks.
  • Access audit insights using natural language prompts.

This conversational experience reduces manual navigation, improves productivity, and enables administrators to quickly access the information required for governance, compliance, and troubleshooting

 


Key Features and Benefits

1. Metadata Comparison Across Systems

  • One of the biggest challenges organizations face is ensuring configuration consistency across Development, Test, and Production environments. Even small differences in metadata configurations can lead to unexpected behavior, deployment issues, or business process disruptions.
  • To address this, the solution provides a comprehensive metadata comparison capability that analyzes configurations across multiple SAP SuccessFactors environments. Users can quickly identify differences at both entity and field levels, including properties such as field type, required status, visibility settings, and other configuration attributes.
  • What makes this capability valuable is the ability to drill down into detailed comparison results while also providing a summarized view for faster analysis. This significantly reduces the effort required to validate configurations and troubleshoot deployment issues.

 

2. Configuration Workbook Generation

  • Maintaining configuration documentation is often a manual and time-consuming task. As SuccessFactors environments evolve, documentation can quickly become outdated, making audits and validations more difficult.
  • The solution automatically generates configuration workbooks directly from SuccessFactors metadata. Users can select specific modules, entities, and fields to create structured Excel-based documentation.
  • This ensures organizations always have access to accurate and up-to-date configuration baselines while significantly reducing manual documentation effort.

 

3. Employee Data Change Audit

  • Tracking employee data changes is a critical requirement for both compliance and operational governance. However, identifying who changed a record, what was changed, and when the change occurred often requires significant manual investigation.
  • The Employee Data Audit capability provides complete visibility into employee-related modifications by capturing change history, event details, modification timestamps, and responsible users.
  • This enables HR and compliance teams to quickly investigate changes, improve accountability, and maintain audit readiness.

 


4. Timesheet Audit

  • Timesheet data plays an important role in workforce management and payroll processes. Organizations often require visibility into timesheet updates, approvals, and historical modifications.
  • The Timesheet Audit feature provides detailed tracking of employee timesheet activities, including modification history, status updates, and audit records.
  • This helps organizations improve transparency while supporting compliance and reporting requirements.

 


5. Benefits Audit

  • Employee benefits data frequently changes throughout the employee lifecycle. Maintaining visibility into these changes is essential for compliance and operational accuracy.
  • The Benefits Audit module enables organizations to track benefit-related updates, view modification history, identify responsible users, and export audit information for reporting purposes.
  • This provides greater transparency and simplifies compliance reviews.


6. Role-Based Permission (RBP) Audit

  • Managing user permissions is one of the most critical aspects of governance and security within SAP SuccessFactors.
  • The RBP Audit capability allows organizations to monitor permission changes, review modified roles, identify responsible administrators, and track historical updates.
  • By providing complete visibility into permission-related activities, organizations can strengthen security controls and support compliance initiatives.


7. Recruitment Auditing

  • Recruitment processes involve continuous activity across job requisitions and candidate applications. Monitoring these activities is important for operational oversight and compliance reporting.
  • The solution provides dedicated auditing capabilities for both Job Applications and Job Requisitions, enabling users to track recruitment activity, review status changes, monitor modification history, and generate reports when needed.
  • This helps organizations maintain transparency throughout the recruitment lifecycle.

 


8. Deleted Records Tracking

  • Deleted employee records can create significant challenges during audits and investigations if proper tracking mechanisms are not available.
  • The solution identifies deleted records and provides details about when the deletion occurred and who performed the action. This helps organizations maintain historical visibility and supports compliance investigations when required.


9. Historical Audit Record Management

  • As audit data grows over time, organizations need efficient ways to manage historical information while complying with retention policies.
  • The solution provides capabilities to review and purge historical audit records in a controlled manner. This helps manage data volume, improve system performance, and maintain compliance with organizational retention requirements.

 

 

10. Business Rule Extraction and Workbook Generation

  • Business rules are a critical component of SuccessFactors implementations, yet documenting them manually can be challenging and error-prone.
  • The solution automatically extracts business rules from the system and generates structured workbooks for documentation and review purposes.
  • This improves documentation accuracy, simplifies governance processes, and reduces ongoing maintenance effort.

Business Value

  • This solution delivers significant value by helping organizations strengthen governance, improve compliance, and reduce manual effort associated with auditing and documentation activities.
  • One of the biggest advantages is improved visibility. Administrators and compliance teams can quickly identify configuration differences across environments, monitor employee data changes, and track critical system activities from a centralized platform.
  • The solution also improves audit readiness by providing detailed tracking of employee updates, permission changes, recruitment activities, and deleted records. Instead of spending hours collecting information from multiple sources, teams can access the required audit information in a single place.
  • Another important benefit is the reduction of manual effort. Tasks such as metadata comparison, workbook generation, business rule extraction, and audit reporting are automated, allowing teams to focus on analysis and decision-making rather than data collection.
  • By providing accurate documentation and complete change visibility, the solution helps organizations improve data quality, maintain configuration consistency, and reduce operational risks across their SAP SuccessFactors landscape.
  • Finally, because the solution is built on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), it offers a secure, scalable, and enterprise-ready foundation that can support organizations of all sizes.

Conclusion
Managing configurations, employee data changes, permissions, and compliance activities across SAP SuccessFactors environments can quickly become a complex and time-consuming task. As organizations grow, maintaining visibility into these activities becomes increasingly important for governance, operational efficiency, and regulatory compliance.

The Audit and Compliance Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM addresses these challenges by providing a centralized platform for metadata comparison, employee data auditing, recruitment tracking, permission monitoring, and automated documentation generation.

From our experience working with SAP SuccessFactors implementations, we have seen how difficult it can be to manually track changes, validate configurations, and prepare for audits. By automating these processes and bringing critical audit information together in a single platform, organizations can significantly reduce effort while improving transparency and control.

Ultimately, this solution is not just about auditing system activities—it is about enabling organizations to operate with greater confidence. By providing complete visibility into changes across the SAP SuccessFactors landscape, organizations can strengthen compliance, improve governance, and focus on driving business value rather than managing manual audit processes.

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation – Rehire Process Design – Part 1
entry.content:
This is part of an ongoing blog series on SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation. In this post, we explore how to handle the rehire process, design options, dependencies, and recommendations. Implementing the right design eliminates manual work, enables reuse of existing employee identifiers, and efficiently supports other requirements. The content of this blog is based purely on my experience. 

  The rehire process in SAP SuccessFactors handles the return of former employees to the organization. Managing this effectively requires balancing data integrity (avoiding duplicate profiles) with process automation across Employee Central and Onboarding. 

 This blog is divided into two parts:  

Part 1 – Covers the rehire options in EC and ONB, requirements validation, dependencies, and rehire check options for existing terminated, inactive employees in Employee Central (EC) and Onboarding.   

Part 2 – Discusses design options and recommendations for detecting historical rehires who are not part of Employee Central. 

 

1) Advantages of Detecting Rehire Records: 

 Identifying rehires before hiring has many advantages; the list is not limited to the following. 

  • Single Source of Truth (No Duplicate Profiles):  
    Prevents the creation of duplicate Person/User IDs and ensures all employment history (performance, training, personal data) remains linked to a single employee record. 
  • Accurate Benefits and Seniority: 
    Ensures correct calculation of tenure-based benefits, seniority, and vesting by retaining original hire date and service history. 
  • Compliance (GDPR): 
    Avoids fragmented personal data across multiple records, making data retention, audit, and “Right to be Forgotten” requests easier and more accurate. 
  • Rehire Eligibility Control: 
    Enforces rehire eligibility checks to prevent onboarding of employees marked as “Not Eligible for Rehire.” 
  • Downstream System Integrity: 
    Prevents duplicate identities across integrated systems (Payroll, Active Directory..etc), avoiding identity and provisioning conflicts. 

 

2)  Rehire Options for terminated employees who have data in SAP SuccessFactors HCM Employee Central:  

 When a terminated record exists in the Employee Central (EC) database, there are two options to rehire an employee in EC/Onboarding (ONB): Rehire with New Employment and Rehire on Old Employment. 

 2.1 Rehire with New Employment 

HR BPs can rehire a former employee by initiating a new employment record. In this flow, a new employment portlet is added to the Employee Record. The Employee Central personal entities—such as National ID, Person ID, and Employee ID—continue from the previous employment. However, new entities for Employment, Job Information, Compensation, and other employment portlets will be added. Additionally, a new Assignment ID and User ID will be generated for the new employment record, differentiating it from previous employment. The employee profile of the rehired worker will include separate, distinct records for both the new and previous employment with the company. 

 

2.2 Rehire with Old Employment 

HR BPs can rehire a former employee based on one of their old employment records with the company. Every old employment is available as a profile to choose during the rehire process. 

 2.3 Comparison of both options and limitations  

Rehire with Old Employment  

Rehire with New Employment  

Updates the existing employment record with new job information. 

Adds a brand-new employment entity linked to the same master Person ID. 

Keeps the original Assignment id/ User ID . 

Generates a new Assignment ID / User ID. 

Seasonal or short-term returns where continuous organizational history matters. 

Long-term rehires returning to a completely different legal entity, country, or department. 

Clean, unified career history layout; preserve historical learning and performance data directly on the profile. 

Perfect separation of data for legal, taxation, and clear tenure/benefit resetting. 

Future-dated terminations aren’t currently supported when rehiring old employment. Future-dated terminations are situations in which the last working day is scheduled for a future date. 

 

Active employees with future-dated termination can be rehired on new employment 

Contingent to Regular, Regular to Contingent Rehires not supported  

Contingent to Regular, Regular to Contingent Rehires supported 

 

3) Requirements Validation for Choosing New Employment vs. Old Employment 

 Validating customer-specific rehire requirements is a critical step in the process. These requirements vary from customer to customer; however, based on experience, the following are the most common use cases that play a key role in rehire process design and decision-making. The first four requirements focus on defining how the rehire process should function when a termination record already exists in the SAP SuccessFactors system. 

 

 

Validating the business’s expected outcome for the rehire in each scenario and then making a final decision. 

  • Regular Terminated Employee: A regular employee who was terminated and is rejoining the organization as a regular employee. 
  • Contingent Employee: A contingent worker who was terminated and is rejoining the organization as a contingent worker. 
  • Regular to Contingent: A regular employee who was terminated and is now rejoining the organization as a contingent worker. 
  • Contingent to Regular: A contingent worker who was terminated and is now rejoining the organization as a regular employee. 
  • Talent-Only Record: Defines rehire behavior when only a talent profile exists in the system, without an Employee Central record. 
  • Integration Requirements and Dependencies: Identifying upstream and downstream system impacts, and the complexity of integration logic. 
  • S/4HANA and EC Payroll: Defining the expected behavior of EC Payroll during PTP replication and S/4HANA BIB replication. 
  • Data Migration Approach: Determining how historical data should be migrated and aligned with rehire scenarios, including any associated challenges. 
  • Identity Management Integration: Ensuring proper handling of user ID reuse, including the reactivation of old Active Directory (AD) profiles during rehire. 
  • Country-Specific and Payroll Requirements: Addressing localization needs, compliance requirements, and payroll system dependencies across different regions. 

  

4) Recruit-to-Hire Process – Rehire Check Options in SAP SuccessFactors HCM:  

As part of the hiring process, rehire detection is triggered across Recruiting, Onboarding, and Employee Central. SuccessFactors (SF ONB and EC) leverage standard capabilities to validate candidate attributes against the Employee Central database to identify returning employees.  

 

 

 

4.1 Rehire Checks in SAP SuccessFactors RCM:  

SAP SuccessFactors RCM does not provide native functionality to compare Job Applicant data against inactive Employee Central profiles to identify potential rehires. 

Even if a custom integration is built to validate against EC data, reliable matching is still challenging because key identifiers such as National ID are often not captured in Recruiting, and in some cases, even date of birth is not available. 

As a result, implementing an efficient and fully accurate rehire detection process in RCM is difficult. However, a practical approach is to explicitly ask candidates whether they have previously worked for the organization. If yes, additional details such as Employee ID and other known attributes can be collected and used to validate the record and flag potential rehires. 

 

4.2 Rehire checks in SAP SuccessFactors HCM Onboarding:            

 Onboarding offers by default two rehire checks in the process, before creating any person in Onboarding, the first rehire check, also known as pre-onboarding rehire verification, is triggered. 

 

 

 

 

Reference: The above diagram is from the SAP to help documentation.  

 When data is passed from Recruiting (RCM) or an external ATS into Onboarding , the system uses the following primary identifiers to run its matching algorithms: 

  • National ID / Government ID 
  • First Name & Last Name 
  • Date of Birth (DOB) 
  • Personal Email Address  

If the new hire isn’t identified as a rehire in the first rehire check, the second rehire check is triggered after the Personal Data Collection step. If the new hire is identified as a rehire after the second rehire check, one of the following apply for rehire with new and old employments:  

  • Rehire with new employment: The onboarding process as rehire is cancelled and restarted with new employment under the old Person Details. 
  • Rehire with old employment: After a rehire coordinator chooses one of the previous employment profiles during the rehire process, the previous employment under the same Person Details is reactivated. 

4.3 Rehiring in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central:  

 Pre-hire records coming from SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central trigger a rehire check within Onboarding. When a hire is made directly in Employee Central, the system performs a rehire check against the Employee Central database. If a potential match is found, the system provides an option to proceed with a rehire to a new employment or to the old employment. 

If no match is found, the system treats the candidate as a new hire. The next blog will cover this topic in detail.

5. Commonly asked questions: 

 

5. 1)How do date fields in Employment Information behave for different rehire options? 

The behavior of the date fields in Employment Information varies depending on whether you rehire old employment or new employment. The following table describes how each date field behaves in these scenarios: 

 

Technical Field 

Rehire on Old Employment 

Rehire on New Employment 

start-date 

start-date 

start-date 

originalStartDate 

originalStartDate 

start-date 

serviceDate 

start-date 

start-date 

seniorityDate 

start-date 

start-date 

benefits-eligibility-start-date 

start-date 

start-date 

5. 2) What is the recommended approach for Rehire?  

It all depends on customer requirements. If there is no impact on payroll, integrations, or other differentiating factors discussed above, then hiring a new employee is cleaner. However, this decision should be carefully evaluated before being finalized. 

5.3 )Have you seen customers using both the “Rehire with Old Employment” and “New Employment” options? 

Yes, many customers adopt a hybrid approach based on use cases. For example, in scenarios such as Contingent-to-Regular or Regular-to-Contingent, they often use Rehire with New Employment. In other scenarios, they may use Rehire with Old Employment.  

5.4)Is the rehire check triggered for records that exist only at the Employee Profile (platform) level? 

It won’t trigger a rehire check. At the same time, when you hire an employee with the same IDs, the system will throw an error stating that a record already exists. Because there is already a platform record with the same user ID.

entry.title: Integration of Position Budgeting Control for Cloud with SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Payroll
entry.content:
Position Budgeting Control (PBC) for Cloud enables organizations to reserve and control workforce budget across fiscal years and budget periods. PBC acts as a bridge between SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central and SAP S/4HANA Financials, ensuring that payroll costs are aligned with approved budgets. This blog describes how to integrate PBC with SAP S/4HANA Payroll in an on-premise or private cloud deployment.

Note: SAP provides standard integration with Employee Central Payroll on the modern stack only. This guide describes the steps to integrate PBC with SAP S/4HANA Payroll. Custom iFlow development and any issues arising from non-standard integration configurations are the responsibility of the implementing partner or customer.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, you must complete the setup and configuration of Position Budgeting Control for Cloud.

High-Level Overview of the PBC Integration to SAP S/4HANA Payroll

Figure 1 Block diagram showing the integration architecture for SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Payroll, highlighting which components are part of the standard integration and which require custom development.

Integrating PBC with SAP S/4HANA Payroll (On-Premise/Private Cloud)

For SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Payroll, the standard synchronization job is not available. Instead, you must build custom iFlows to handle synchronization of symbolic account and employee grouping data, and to communicate with the earmarked funds API.

Build Custom iFlows for Symbolic Accounts and Employee Groupings

The symbolic account and employee grouping services cannot use standard communication scenarios for on-premise systems and must be handled via custom iFlows. Create custom iFlows to consume the following standard OData services delivered by SAP:

  • HRSFEC_SYMBOLIC_ACCOUNT_SRV – Fetches symbolic account data from payroll.
  • HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV – Fetches employee grouping data for account determination.

Activate the services in your SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system and enable them with Basic Auth or OAuth-based configuration.

End-to-End iFlow Implementation

In this integration, a single iFlow calls both OData services sequentially within SAP Integration Suite (CPI), captures each response, and combines the data before returning the final response to PBC.

Overview

  • User triggers the iFlow using an HTTPS sender.
  • The iFlow calls HRSFEC_SYMBOLIC_ACCOUNT_SRV, captures its response.
  • The iFlow then calls HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV in a similar manner.
  • The combined response is returned.

Step 1: Create an iFlow

Create a new Integration Flow in SAP Integration Suite with the following structure:

HTTPS sender → iFlow logic → HTTP receiver

Step 2: Configure the HTTPS Sender Adapter

In the sender adapter, set the address in the Connection tab (endpoint path). This endpoint is what you will configure later in Security Center for symbolic account and employee grouping. For example: /SymbolicAccount. The address must start with /.

Step 3: Configure the Content Modifier

Add a Content Modifier step and configure the required exchange properties for the API calls (such as the target system URL and authentication parameters).

Step 4: Set the ERP Configuration

Configure the connection details pointing to your SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system.

Step 5: Add a Request Reply Step

Add a Request Reply step to handle the outbound HTTP call to the on-premise OData service.

Step 6: Configure the HTTP Adapter (SAP S/4HANA On-Premise System)

Configure the HTTP receiver adapter with the endpoint of the on-premise OData service (HRSFEC_SYMBOLIC_ACCOUNT_SRV or HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV) and the appropriate authentication method.

Repeat Steps 3–6 to add the second OData service call (HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV) within the same iFlow.

Step 7: Save and Deploy the iFlow

Save and deploy the iFlow in SAP Integration Suite. After deployment, verify the status in the Integration Content Monitor — the iFlow should show as Started. Any startup errors will be visible here before any message processing log is created.

Step 8: Test the iFlow

Test the iFlow using the service bindings created for the instance. Confirm that both OData services return data as expected.

After building and testing the iFlows, configure the synchronization job with the iFlow endpoint details and register the iFlows in Security Center, as shown in Configuring the Integration Content for the Earmarked Funds API and Configuring the X509 Certificate in the Integration Flow.

Alternative approach: Instead of building custom iFlows to retrieve the symbolic account and employee grouping data, you can use the Manage Symbolic Accounts and Manage Employee Groupings APIs during configuration to create and save the mapping between the Pay Components ID to Symbolic Accounts ID and Employee Class and Employment Type to Employee Grouping for account determination. The symbolic accounts and employee groupings from the payroll systems are used by Position Budgeting and Control to derive the G/L account information required for the Financials system in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

These APIs expose the same data and can be used when a custom iFlow is not preferred:

  • Symbolic Accounts API
  • Employee Groupings API

Enable PBC in SAP S/4HANA Payroll (On-Premise/Private Cloud)

Register the symbolic account and account determination APIs configured in the previous steps. The enabling, registering, and setup steps may differ from the standard Employee Central Payroll steps detailed in the Implementing Employee Central Payroll based on SAP Human Capital Management for SAP S/4HANA guide.

Perform IT724 Replication

Ensure the PTP Replication connection is configured using report RP_HRSFEC_PTP_CONFIGURATION as a prerequisite. Then schedule report RP_HRSFEC_PBC_REPL (transaction HRSFEC_PBC_REPL) as a periodic job in the payroll system. This report fetches financial status and line items via the PBC API and replicates the data to IT724 in your payroll system. Application logs are written under object HRSFEC, sub-object PBC, with external ID prefix IT724_REPLICATION*.

entry.title: Employee Pay Transparency in SAP SuccessFactors: Turning Localization Into an Executable Process
entry.content:
Employee pay transparency has rapidly moved from a policy discussion to an operational expectation. This shift is driven by evolving pay equity rules worldwide—such as established gender pay gap reporting frameworks in the UK and Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO) reporting dimensions in the United States—coupled with the recent June 7, 2026, EU Directive transposition deadline. However, the underlying challenge is a moving target: because each country finalizes its legislation in slightly different ways, multinational companies must honor a fragmented set of local regulations. Rather than relying on rigid global schemas, organizations need a way to explain pay in a structured, consistent, and auditable way. In SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, these universal requirements are addressed through generic utility frameworks designed explicitly to balance global data stability with local compliance variants.

A Structured Approach to Pay Transparency Data

At the center of this capability is a standardized way to store and manage both individual employee pay data and aggregated comparator data. Rather than enforcing hardcoded global master data structures, the solution introduces dedicated Metadata Framework (MDF) objects that separate annual compliance records from transactional day-to-day data.
Two supporting objects play a key role: the Employee Pay Snapshot captures a comprehensive picture for a defined calendar year, while the Work Category Pay Snapshot stores aggregated averages and medians for comparable employee groups. Together, they form the factual basis for transparent and legally defensible pay communication.
 

Configuration Foundations Matter

Before any documents can be generated, the Employee Pay Transparency Information Object must be configured correctly. This includes assigning appropriate permissions, ensuring access to both snapshot objects, and activating the Document Generation framework. From a governance perspective, this step is not merely technical—it defines who can see, generate, and distribute sensitive pay information. This proper setup ensures data can be maintained dynamically through structured framework tools or imports, which is essential in multi-country landscapes.
 

From Data to Document

Once the foundation is in place, the system enables the generation of Employee Pay Information Documents using standard SuccessFactors Document Generation capabilities. Available via secure HR administration or employee self-service, these reports employ rule-based data selection and calendar-year logic to ensure the correct records are evaluated.
 

Scaling with People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud

Fulfilling individual employee requests is only the baseline. True pay equity requires an undisputed system of record drawing directly from live payroll runs, not just estimated compensation fields. Through SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC), pay data is segmented into fixed, variable, and complementary pay.
This data foundation flows directly into Pay Transparency Insights, a built-in capability within People Intelligencein SAP Business Data Cloud. By delivering advanced data science capabilities like prebuilt regression analysis apps, HR executives can confidently drill down across gender, pay grade, and legal entity dimensions to statistically identify and justify pay gaps before they turn into compliance risks.
 

Final Thoughts

Pay transparency is not achieved through a single report or feature; it is an ongoing operational capability that must scale over time. While final legislation continues to solidify across different jurisdictions, the most critical prerequisite is auditing your master data quality today. Ensuring disciplined data modeling and reliable payroll configurations guarantees that your organization is fully prepared to navigate evolving regional laws and earn employee trust through consistency and clarity.
 

Further references

  • Data Models for Pay Transparency Insights
  • Employee Pay Transparency Information
  • Pay Transparency Insights
  • The EU Pay Transparency Deadline Is Coming: What HR Leaders Need to Get Right Before June 7
  • International Women’s Day 2026: Building Trust and Equity Through Pay Transparency
  • SAP Introduces New Capabilities to Advance Pay Equity and Help Ensure EU Pay Transparency Compliance

entry.title: Candidate Rehire Check from SmartRecruiters when integrating with SuccessFactors
entry.content:
The purpose of this article is to explain how the candidate rehires checks work when you send a candidate from SmartRecruiters to SuccessFactors.  It is particularly aimed towards Smart Recruiters consultants who may not have a deep understanding of SuccessFactors Onboarding or Employee Central.

To begin with let’s recap on the rehire check for external applicants.  Let’s assume we have SuccessFactors Recruitment, Onboarding and Employee Central enabled.

1st Rehire Check

This is triggered when you ‘Initiate Onboarding’ from SuccessFactors Recruitment.  It checks a combination of four values based on the settings in SuccessFactors Employee Central > Manage Data> Hire/Rehire Configuration.

This is typically controlled by the Employee Central team and uses, 4 fields, first, last name, DOB and the values in National ID portlet.  You have these options and they can’t be adjusted to included other options.  These are the only rehire options and are the same in ALL rehire checks.  Whether you rehire on old or new employment the rehire check is the same.

If it finds an inactive employee in SuccessFactors Employee Central, then this will trigger a rehire check before SuccessFactors Onboarding processes are initiated.  The onboarding configuration will decide who conducts the rehire check.  Once the check is completed the onboarding process continues.

2nd rehire check

This is triggered in SuccessFactors Onboarding when the candidate completes the stage Personal Data Collection.  The onboarding configuration will decide who conducts the rehire check.  Once the check is completed the onboarding process continues.

3rd Rehire Check

This is triggered in Manage Pending Hires and occurs when you skip onboarding for a hire or when you do not have onboarding enabled.  The system uses the same rehire logic and the rehire check is completed by the person processing the hire.

SmartRecruiters to Onboarding.

When you initiate onboarding from SmartRecruiters the 1st Rehire check is not initiated.  You only have the 2nd Rehire check.  If the check is missed, then the 3rd rehire check will run in MPH.

SmartRecruiters to Employee Central (MPH)

When you send the data to MPH the 1st rehire check is not initiated, neither is 2nd rehire check as this sits within onboarding.  You just have the 3rd rehire check.

 

As internal hires currently do not flow from SmartRecruiters to SuccessFactors then I have not confused matters here by talking about rehire checks for ‘internal active employees with future date termination dates’ etc.  I will address those once there is an active flow for internal hires.

Finally I am aware that in 2027 Onboarding product management does have a roadmap item to make the rehire check flexible and to be able to move it to different parts of the process.  I think this would very good enhancement and I am particularly excited about it!

entry.title: Lessons from a Phase 2 SAP SF Rollout:-Governance, migration control, and partner accountability
entry.content:
About This Piece

I had the opportunity to work as the functional and technical Lead consultant on this engagement — a Phase 2 Employee Central and Time Off rollout that extended an already-live SAP SuccessFactors instance to two new countries in South-East Asia. My part in it covered configuration build, migration planning, UAT coordination, Production deployment along with working closely with the client's IT Governance & HR team and an existing AMS partner. It was a good learning experience, and the points below come straight from that project, not from a textbook.

Phase 2 Is a Different Animal

When you build SuccessFactors from scratch, there is always some room to adjust as you go along. Phase 2 does not give you that luxury. The instance is already live, real people are using it in other countries, and a change to a shared object — a Corporate Data Model, a global Business Rule, a Position structure, leave object etc— touches every region on the system, not just the ones you are rolling out to.

A few things make Phase 2 that much more demanding:

  • The system is already running Core HR ESS, MSS process including payroll and leave transactions for live users elsewhere, so mistakes have immediate consequences.
  • Shared objects belong to everyone on the instance, so even a small, well-intentioned change can ripple outward.
  • The stakeholder circle is wider too — IT Governance, AMS, HR teams from multiple countries, and the implementation team all need to know exactly where their role begins and ends.

Four Things Worth Getting Right

1. Lock Governance Early, and Keep It Locked

On this engagement, a bundle-only migration rule was introduced two-thirds of the way through build, without an impact assessment or a timeline review. It cost us roughly two weeks of rework, and a fair bit of back-and-forth to get everyone aligned again. It taught us, quite plainly, that governance decided upfront is worth far more than governance revisited midway.

What we would recommend:

Lock governance, RACI, and the migration approach during Discovery, before a single configuration object is built. If the approach must change later, let it go through a proper impact assessment and sign-off first.

2. Not All Configuration Objects Are the Same

Standard, country-specific configuration moves through a bundle smoothly — you build it, transport it, validate it, done. Shared global objects need a gentler hand. On this project, a Corporate Data Model change, Position object change, leave object change meant for the new countries had to be manually checked against the live configuration already used by three other regions before we could touch it.

What we would recommend:

Use a hybrid migration model. Let standard, country-scoped configuration e.g. country specific data model changes, country specific custom objects, benefits, Leaves etc go through Transport Management as usual, and handle shared global objects manually, with a documented comparison against Production each time.

3. Draw Clear Lines Between Partners

Most Phase 2 programs bring together at least four parties — the Implementation Partner, the HR team, the AMS partner, and the client's IT Governance team. On our project, the AMS partner was set up for support-level work, but ended up in an implementation-grade role e.g. all UAT migration to be done with implementation consultants on call. Our consultants stayed engaged during migration process explaining  migration tracker , bundles, migration fixes, defect fixes, to help out, time that could otherwise have gone into test scripts, user guides, and proper knowledge transfer.

What we would recommend:

Agree on a simple competency matrix upfront. Implementation owns build, migration, and UAT fixes; AMS can shadow from UAT if they are ready for it, and takes over formally from Hypercare onwards.

4. Treat Data Migration as Implementation Work, Not an Admin Task

Data migration deserves the same seriousness as configuration build. On this engagement, ownership of the Production upload shifted twice — first over data privacy concerns, then over readiness —during UAT it was done by AMS partner under the guidance of Implementation consultant. During production migration, process got changed, it was initially given to HR before it settled with the implementation Consultants.

What we would recommend:

Fix data ownership, access, and privacy approvals at least 60 days before Go-Live, and make sure whoever runs the Production upload has already done at least one full mock load in QAS. Please note that Core HR data migration is a significant activity that involves uploading data across more than 20 templates. Therefore, the technical competency of the nominated resource should be thoroughly assessed before deciding whether to assign the activity to the HR team or the AMS partner.

 

A Quick Word on UAT

UAT on a shared instance genuinely needs more care, since QAS is shared too. But care should not mean everything grinds to a halt. On our project, one small, three-field configuration fix took two full days to move through the standard approval cycle. That experience is what led the team to introduce a same-day Emergency Fix Protocol for low-risk defects, with AMS reviewing the change rather than gatekeeping it. It made a real difference to morale during a demanding phase of the project.

What We Learned: Summary

Area

What Happened

What Helped

Governance

Migration approach changed mid-build, with no impact review.

Lock governance and RACI during Discovery; any later change goes through a formal impact assessment.

Migration Strategy

A bundle-only rule was applied even to shared global objects.

Use a hybrid model — standard config via Transport Management, shared objects handled manually.

Partner Roles

AMS was scoped for support but stepped into an implementation-grade role.

Agree a competency matrix upfront; AMS formally takes over from Hypercare.

Data Ownership

Upload ownership shifted twice, quite late in the project.

Lock ownership 60+ days out; require a full QAS mock load before Production. Assess technical competency of the nominated role for Data upload.

UAT Agility

Every fix, big or small, went through the full approval cycle.

Introduce an Emergency Fix Protocol for low-risk defects.

A Final Thought

Phase 2 programs bring their own kind of pressure, but they also bring some of the best team moments — the late evenings sorted out together, the small wins that nobody outside the project ever hears about. Most of what we have shared here is entirely preventable, simply by getting a few things right before execution begins rather than fixing them in the middle of it.

These lessons come from genuinely being in the room when things got a little difficult, and figuring our way through them as a team. If you are heading into a Phase 2 SuccessFactors rollout and want to think through governance, migration, or your partner model, we would be happy to have that conversation. And if you have your own lessons from a similar journey, do share them — that is exactly the kind of thing the SAP community grows stronger from.

Thank you!

Mohammad Saddam Aquib

entry.title: 11. SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation - Rehire Process Design for Historical Termination Records
entry.content:
This is part of an ongoing blog series on SAP SuccessFactors' HCM implementation. In this post, we explore how to design the Rehire Process for Historical Termination Records that are not part of EC. In the previous blog, Part 1, I covered rehire options in EC and ONB, requirements validation, dependencies, and rehire check options for existing terminated or inactive employees in Employee Central (EC) and Onboarding. The complex problem every SAP SuccessFactors HCM customer faces is how to detect rehires who are not in Employee Central.

 

This blog is part 2 – it discusses design options and recommendations for detecting historical rehires who are not in Employee Central.

 

Customer Requirement Scenario:

 

A customer has been running a legacy HR system for more than 25 years and is now implementing SAP SuccessFactors HCM. As part of the data migration strategy, only the top-of-stack employee data is being migrated to Employee Central (EC), which aligns with SAP's leading practice recommendation. As a result, approximately 25 years of terminated employee records will remain in the legacy system and will not be available in EC after go-live.

The customer requested a detailed solution design to identify, and process rehires for these historical terminated employees after the SAP SuccessFactors EC goes live.

As discussed in my previous blog, when a terminated employee record exists in Employee Central, the standard rehire checks in SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding (ONB) and Employee Central automatically detect a potential match and provide HR administrators with the option to Rehire on Old Employment or Rehire with New Employment, depending on the business scenario.

However, if the terminated employee record is not available in Employee Central, the system treats the individual as a new hire by default, resulting in the creation of new Person IDs, User IDs, Employment IDs, and Employment records. For organizations that want to preserve employee history, maintain compliance, or recognize prior service, this behavior may not meet their business requirements.

Based on my implementation experience, I have successfully designed and implemented three approaches to address this requirement. Among these, the first option is my preferred and most strongly recommended approach because it is simple, scalable, cost-effective, and aligns well with SAP SuccessFactors architecture.

 

Solution Design  1) Migrate historical terminated records with a minimum set of data/portlets:

The cleanest approach is migrating all termination records with a minimum set of data. As part of the migration, we identify terminated records and load them with a minimal data set after discussing with the business which attributes are critical; the remaining mandatory attributes will use default values. While this adds additional effort to the data migration scope, it makes the Rehire process very clean.

This is the recommended approach, in my view. If a customer has thousands of terminated records and migrating them as part of the Employee Central (EC) implementation requires significant effort, they could migrate them after EC is live. However, I strongly recommend doing it as part of the initial EC migration itself.

 

Since we are only going to load a few templates (Biographical, Personal, Address, Email, National ID, Employment, and Job Info) with a minimal set of real values and default values.

 

If you are migrating data from S/4HANA or SAP ERP HCM via Info Porter, you can set up a separate mapping template for terminated employees and load those records accordingly.

If you are migrating data manually using import templates or other migration tools, make sure you have the right logic in place to generate the correct data.

Data Migration Process for Terminated Records

Points to Remember: 

  • This solution adds extra effort to data migration, and that needs to be considered.
  • Bring only the top-of-stack termination record and add the hire record.
  • Try to populate only the minimum set of required fields in Job Info, use default values for foundation object attributes (Position, Department, Division, Pay Grade, Cost Center..etc).
  • If migrating all historical terminations is not an option, consider how far back you go for rehires. Based on that, migrate historical termination records.

 

Solution Design 2) Create an MDF object and load historical termination records:

This is another approach I have used in the past: when migrating historical termination records is not an option due to a high volume of records, we create a custom MDF object to maintain a minimum set of data. This includes attributes such as First Name, Last Name, Date of Birth, National ID, Termination Date, Email, and other minimum required fields.

 

  1. Migrate historical former employee data into a Historical Former Employees MDF object.
  2. If an external recruiting system is used, it can call the Historical Former Employees MDF Object OData API to identify potential rehires during the recruiting process.
  3. In SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting (RCM), a rehire check can be implemented using the MDF object to detect potential rehires before the hiring process progresses.
  4. Once onboarding is initiated, build a process to validate the employee against the Historical Former Employees MDF object, flag potential rehires, and trigger the appropriate action. This can be implemented using a business rule.
  5. For employees hired directly in Employee Central (EC), a business rule can be configured during the hire process to check the Historical Former Employees MDF object, flag potential rehires, and enable the HR administrator to take the appropriate rehire action.

 

Solution Design 3)Build a process to look up the external system where historical HR data resides:

 

This is the third option we have used to perform a rehire check. Assume the customer is moving from S4HANA to SAP SuccessFactors HCM, will use S4HANA for payroll, and will keep complete historical HR Data.

 

The above options are not feasible for various reasons, i have used this design to perform a lookup in the S4HANA system to check for rehire. If any matches are found, it returns the details with the Employee ID and other attributes. Based on this, you take action.

 

 

  • Create an OData or SOAP service in SAP S/4HANA that returns historical employee data based on input parameters. Expose the service through SAP API Management so that it can be securely consumed by external systems.
  • If an external recruiting system is used, it can call the SAP S/4HANA OData service to identify potential rehires during the recruiting process.
  • In SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting (RCM) or SmartRecruiters, a rehire Check can be implemented by calling the SAP S/4HANA OData service through SAP Integration Suite, triggered by Intelligent Services events.
  • Once onboarding is initiated, SAP Integration Suite can invoke the SAP S/4HANA OData service to validate whether the individual is a potential rehire, flag the record, and trigger the appropriate rehire action ( Rehire with old employment /New Employment).

entry.title: PY-GB: Mandating of Payrolling of Benefits from April 2027 – Update
entry.content:
On 15th June HMRC notified SAP that the mandating of payrolling of benefits will now be a phased roll out instead of big bang approach.

As the phased roll out includes Medical Benefits from April 2027 SAP still anticipate significant changes will be required. Currently non car benefits are processed in a very simplistic way based on the values input on IT0014 or 0015. Our understanding is that this processing will need to be extended so that annual amounts of benefit are adjusted over the remaining pay periods in the tax year to meet HMRCs requirements. The changes required to support the processing of medical benefit will then provide the framework for all subsequent non car related benefits when they too are mandated for payrolling and reporting via the FPS. 

Key changes (not an exhaustive list): 

  • Update processing for non car benefits to use annual values and remaining payroll periods in the calculation of per period adjustments. 
  • Ability to identify benefits as applicable for Class 1A NICs and map to relevant FPS data items 
  • Even though 94 RTI data items have been removed for the April 2027 scope there still needs to be 32 PBIK fields added to the RTI table and FPS report.  
  • The RTI data items for the reporting of PBIK Class 1A NICs contributions have not been removed from scope so it appears that these will  be reported through the FPS from April 2027 rather than via the P11D(b) as they are currently. This includes both mandated and voluntary payrolled benefits.  
  • The P11D process will need to be updated to remove company cars, vans and medical benefits and exclude PBIK from the Class 1A NICs for tax year 2027-28. SAP anticipate that changes will be required to the P11D form for completion by July 2028 (2027-28 tax year) 

 

SAP has reviewed the outstanding questions we have with HMRC and most remain valid and require clarification. Hopefully once the updated guidance is published by HMRC in July it will provide the clarifications required by SAP and we will be in the position to provide further updates on the planned changes required for April 2027. 

 Please also see the UKI Finance And HCM Localisation workzone page  Migration from P11D to PBIK

entry.title: One Time Type, Multiple Dependent : A Scalable alternate approach to EU Parental Leave Tracking
entry.content:
Introduction 

Managing parental leave in line with country-specific legislation can be complex, especially when eligibility and entitlement must be tracked per child and adjusted based on cumulative leave usage. This blog explains one approach to support EU-style parental leave requirements in SAP SuccessFactors Time Off using Business Rules, MDF objects, Intelligent Services Center (ISC), and Integration Center. 

Business Requirements 

Organizations operating in the EU region may face requirements where parental leave entitlement must be monitored separately for each dependent, with limits applied at the child level. 

Eligibility & Entitlement Rules 

  • Parental leave can be taken until the child's 6th birthday. 
  • Maximum entitlement is 36 WEEKS per child. 
  • The full entitlement may be utilized at any point before the child reaches age 6. 

There are already useful SAP Community references that explain how child-based leave scenarios can be handled in SuccessFactors Time Management: 

Parental Leave Ireland Solution Proposal in SuccessFactors Time Management  

Child Care Leave China Workaround in SuccessFactors   

While these approaches work well for many scenarios, some clients prefer a simpler employee experience: one parental leave time type, a dependent dropdown, and immediate visibility of the leave already consumed for the selected child. 

This blog outlines one possible design to meet that requirement using a single time type, dependent-specific tracking, automated balance updates, and audit-friendly MDF records. 

Custom Fields Added to the Time Type 

Custom fields were introduced on Employee Time to capture dependent information, show consumed leave, and support balance calculations. 

  1. User Field 

Field: cust_userId (Read only)

Although Employee Time already stores the user context, this additional field is used to filter dependent records from the custom Dependent MDF object. 

  1. Dependent Selection 

Field: cust_SelectedChildLeavefor (Editable)

A Generic Object reference field is used to select the dependent for whom parental leave is being requested. The dependent data is copied from the standard dependent portlet into a custom MDF object which is reference in this field. 

  1. Dependent ID, Dependent Name, and Child Date of Birth (Optional) 

Field: cust_dependentID , cust_BirthDateofChild etc. (read only)

These fields are optional - the additional data copied from dependent portlet for dependent is reflected in this fields for view purpose only i.e., Date of birth , Age etc. derived through business rules. 

  1. Number of Days Taken 

Field: cust_NumberofDaysTaken (Read only)

This field is Updated from the MDF tracking table for the selected employee-child combination using business rules.

  1. Delta Days 

Field: cust_DeltaDays (Read only )

This field Stores the difference between the original request and an edited request in case the number of days are changed.

Fields highlighted in red are the key fields used for calculation and tracking. 

Fields highlighted in yellow are display fields populated from MDF when a dependent is selected. 

Dependents Data on the Time Off UI 

Before configuring the parental leave logic, dependent data for children must be copied from the dependent portlet into a custom MDF object through Integration Center. Please refer to the blog -   Parental Leave Ireland Solution Proposal in SuccessFactors Time Management 

How to reference the MDF in the employee time object and how to make the values of specific dependent for each user ? 

1. We are using a custom field - cust_User ID

The custom field cust_User ID stores the employee ID when the time type is selected. Keeping this field visible and read-only on UI is important; otherwise, dependent records for other employees may also appear in the dropdown. 

Business rule set on onChange of time type to set this custom field -

2. Reference Custom MDF (generic object) of copied dependents for user to select on UI.

The dependent dropdown is filtered based on the cust_userId using field criteria, as shown in the screenshot above. 

The Time Off UI then allows employees to select the relevant child and view dependent-specific information on the same request screen. 

Here is the small peep into how time off UI looks after this  -  

Other business rules on Employee time 

  1. Set "Number of days taken" value

     Rule Name : ECTM_MDF_ET_EVAL_SET_POL_UPL_BAL                                                            Trigger : On Change 

    Purpose

  • Reads values from the Parental Leave entries MDF object
  • Retrieves leave already taken for the employee-child combination
  • Updates the Number of Days Taken field

This provides real-time visibility into the leave already consumed for a specific child.

 

       2.   Set the "delta days" field  

              Rule name : ECTM_MDF_ET_EVAL_SET_POL_UPL_DELTA

               Trigger: On Save

              Purpose:

  • Compares the original leave request with the edited request
  • Calculates the difference in days
  • Updates the cust_DeltaDays field

This ensures entitlement balances remain accurate even when a pending request is modified.

           3. Trigger Intelligent services  

              Rule name : ECTM_MDF_ET_VAL_TRIGGER_ISC 

              Trigger : On Save

            Purpose:

  • Publishes an Intelligent Services event 
  • Initiates downstream Integration Center jobs 
  • Keeps the MDF tracking table synchronized with Time Off transactions

MDF Structure for Tracking Leave Usage

A dedicated MDF object for:  Parental Leave – Entries has been created to maintain leave tracker at child level.

Configuration Location

Configure Object Definitions → Search for  Parental Leave – Entries

Data Storage

Manage Data → Search for  Parental Leave – Entries

 Stored Information (fields in MDF)

  • Employee ID 
  • Dependent ID 
  • Child Date of Birth 
  • Number of parental leave days taken 
  • Time Off request reference 

This MDF keeps a essential audit trail and enables accurate entitlement calculations per child. 

Business rule in MDF 

1. Set Parental leave Days taken per child (including DELTA) 

Rule Name : ECTM_MDF_POL_UPL_SET_BALANCE  

Trigger: On Save of the MDF Object 

Purpose: 

  • Aggregates leave values received from Integration Center 
  • Maintains a consolidated leave balance for each employee-child combination 
  • Ensures entitlement tracking remains current 

Intelligent Services Center (ISC) Integration 

The solution leverages SAP SuccessFactors Intelligent Services Center (ISC) to automate Parental Leave entries -MDF updates. 

How ISC Works

ISC follows an Event–Action–Listener framework.

When an event occurs on an object (such as Time Off), ISC listens for the event and triggers predefined actions.

Event Publishing

For Parental Leave, a rule is configured on employee time object (reference above) to publish an event whenever a Time Off request is saved.

Once the event is published: ISC receives the event -> The relevant Integration Center job is triggered.  -> Parental leave entries- MDF data is updated automatically.

The Integration Center jobs therefore use the trigger type: Intelligent Services

In Intelligent Services, save and assign the integration as shown below. 

 

Submission, Approval, Cancellation, and Decline Handling 

On Leave Submission 

The business requirement was to reserve and track entitlement as soon as a request is submitted rather than waiting for final approval. This ensures:

  • Accurate visibility of utilized entitlement
  • Prevention of over-allocation
  • Consistent tracking across multiple requests

Handling Cancellations and Declines

Because leave is recorded upon submission, the solution must also account for situations where a request is: Cancelled or Declined

Separate ISC-triggered Integration Center processes handle these events and:

  • Reverse previously recorded leave usage
  • Update the MDF tracking table
  • Ensure entitlement balances remain accurate. 

Integration Design Overview 

Hence , as to cater submission and cancellation scenarios - there are two integrations that are required so cumulative leave usage remains accurate for the selected child. 

Integration Configuration 

The Integration Center job was configured with the following key settings: 

Setting Value 

Trigger Type 

Intelligent Services 

Source Type 

SuccessFactors 

Destination Type 

SuccessFactors 

Format 

OData 

 Source Object 

Employee Time is used as the source entity and provides info like employee, dependent selected , leave quantity, request status, and request reference information required for entitlement tracking

Destination Object 

The destination is the Parental Leave – Entries MDF object, which stores child-level leave usage and provides traceability back to the originating Time Off request. 

Map the fields as shown below. 

Handling Multiple Approval Statuses 

For approved or pending requests, the leave quantity is added to the balance. For cancelled or declined requests, the previously added quantity is reversed. This is handled through separate integrations with the same mapping logic, except that the cancellation or decline integration multiplies the quantity by -1. 

Finally, assign the integration to Intelligent Services so the correct integration is triggered based on the request action. 

Go to Intelligent Services, select Custom Activities > Integration, and choose the relevant integration. 

Set the event timing to When event is published. 

Select the edit icon and choose Add Rule. 

For the approved or pending integration, configure the rule to trigger when approval status is Approved or Pending, as shown below. 

Save the configuration. The Intelligent Services event and Integration Center job are now linked. 

  Data Flow 

Employee Time → ISC Event → Integration Center → Parental Leave MDF → Balance Recalculation Rule. This flow keeps parental leave balances synchronized and available for future entitlement validation.  

Handling Request Updates 

Request modifications are handled through the Delta Days mechanism, so only the incremental change is reflected in the tracked entitlement and duplicate counting is avoided. 

Final Touch ----

Take rule to check in the span of 6 years of child birth - restrict employee from taking more 36 weeks (180 days) using a take rule.

Take rule to restrict leave application after DOB + 72 MONTHS (6 YRS) as well added to complete the business req. above.

 

Solution Benefits 

Complies with  parental leave legislation 

Tracks entitlement at individual child level via single time type 

Supports request changes through delta calculations 

Provides complete auditability (Use MDF for reporting)

Automates balance updates using Intelligent Services and Integration Center 

Ensures accurate handling of submissions, edits, cancellations, and declines 

Gives employees visibility into leave already consumed for each child 

Conclusion 

By combining SAP SuccessFactors Time Off, MDF objects, Business Rules, Intelligent Services Center, and Integration Center, this solution provides a structured and auditable framework for managing child-based parental leave. It supports entitlement tracking per child, improves employee visibility, and reduces manual administration through automation. 

The same design principle can be adapted for other country-specific leave scenarios where entitlement must be tracked against a dependent, family member, or similar reference object. We used it for family leave for USA where leaves are calculated based on rolling year. Storing the first start of the leave and for specific dependent etc.

Note : This is one possible approach and may need further enhancement depending on client-specific legislation, approval workflows, and reporting requirements. While the solution provides robust child-level entitlement tracking using a single Time Off type and standard SuccessFactors capabilities, successful implementation depends on effective dependent data synchronization, reliable event processing, comprehensive testing of request lifecycle scenarios, and clear reporting and governance processes. Careful consideration of country-specific legislation and customer approval workflows is essential to ensure the solution meets both compliance and operational requirements.

entry.title: Insight into how SmartRecruiters may work for SuccessFactors Internal hires
entry.content:
In this article we will discuss how the SmartRecruiters integration roadmap implies how internal hires will work for SuccessFactors Internal hires.  There are many different types of internal hires.

As of July 15th, Internal hires from SmartRecruiters will not be sent from SmartRecruiters to SuccessFactors.  However, there is now an indication of how this will work in the roadmap. The roadmap states:

‘An internal hire flag is passed to EC/Onboarding and surfaced in MPH to distinguish from external hires and handle them appropriately in EC.
