EC remains the source for creating or adjusting the internal move / future-dated action (e.g. job/position change) on the employee record; that part is not driven automatically from SR.’

This implies that all internal hires will be sent to MPH and once we update the Employee Central record, we can trigger a crossboarding process in Onboarding.  This is NOT vastly different to what happens at the moment.  However, this is only 1 type of internal hire.  There are 5 key types of internal hires:

1. Employee moves with the same country and legal entity.

This is covered in the example above; their Employee Central record is updated first and then we trigger onboarding process if required.  Normally Onboarding is not triggered as most of their data remains the same, the only updates are to job and compensation.  In my opinion based on SmartRecruiters roadmap in Q3 this is not an issue.

2. Employee applies for a job in another country and transfers to that country.

In this example the employee needs to terminate their current employment and be rehired into the new country.  With SuccessFactors recruitment the employee is sent to Onboarding and a  ‘rehire on new employment’ task is created in Onboarding, once this is completed onboarding is started and once this is completed they will land in MPH.  

Sending them into first MPH will not work as we will need new data from the employee, for example the address in the new country, new social security number etc.  In addition Employee Central may have lots of rules forcing them to have this data before you hire them out of MPH.  Even if you can bypass the rules the candidate will be in Employee Central with incomplete data and payroll replication may fail.   Based on what we know in Q3 this is a gap with SmartRecruiters integration.

3. Employee is currently employed as a contingent worker and applied for a permanent position.

In this example the contingent worker can’t just be flipped to an employee in Employee Central.  They need to have their contingent worker record terminated and rehired into the permanent role.  This is very similar to point 2.  Sending them into first MPH will not work. The new record will need new data,  for example, the address, a social security number, a DOB attributes that are missing on a CW record.  As a result we will have an incorrect employee record and this could cause payroll replication to fail.  Based on what we know in Q3 this is a gap with SmartRecruiters.

4. Candidate applies for a role and is sent on a Global Assignment

The current processes with SuccessFactors recruitment send it to MPH and once the Global Assignment is added in Employee Central it triggers an onboarding process.  What we need to ensure is that the employment is marked as a Global Assignment and not just an internal hire.  We need to ensure SmartRecruiters can distinguish those on a Global Assignment.

5. Candidate applies for a role and is sent on a Concurrent Employment

The current processes with SuccessFactors recruitment send it to MPH and once the Concurrent Employment is added in Employee Central it triggers an onboarding process.  As with Global Assignments we need to ensure that the hire is marked as a Concurrent Employment and not just an internal hire.  We need to ensure SmartRecruiters can distinguish those on a Concurrent Employment.

Whilst the roadmap give some indication of how it will work there are still many unanswered questions.  Once i know more i will update this blog.

entry.title: What Disappears in 2H 2026: SAP SuccessFactors Basic Auth & Direct Corporate IdP Integration
entry.content:
The single sentence

With the 2H 2026 release, SAP deletes three login-side capabilities from SAP SuccessFactors — UI Basic Authentication, third-party corporate IdP direct integration, and Partial Organization SSO — plus support for multiple asserting parties wired directly to SuccessFactors (KBA 3472405) [1][2][3][4]. After that, there is no direct authentication into SAP SuccessFactors — every login must flow through SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service (IAS), either as the main IdP or as a proxy in front of your corporate IdP [1][12].

 

The Preview-first trap most plans miss

Preview data centers lose Basic Authentication and direct-IdP login on October 12, 2026, roughly a month before Production [12]. Preview/Test is normally where you rehearse the migration. So:

If you haven't migrated your Preview tenant to IAS by mid-October 2026, you can lose basic-auth access to your own test environment before you've finished testing the migration you were going to run in Production.

The practical sequencing consequence: treat Preview as its own deadline (October 12), not as a warm-up you can leave until October. Migrate and validate Preview well before mid-October, so Preview is already on IAS and available to test against while you prepare Production for November 13.

 

What is deleted with the 2H 2026 release

Four distinct things, all under this one release. Be precise about each: they fail differently and you check for them differently.

1. Basic Authentication for the UI

End users logging into SuccessFactors with a username and password held in SuccessFactors itself, the "company ID + username + password" login screen. This is the classic native login.

What breaks: anyone who authenticates this way can no longer log in. They must instead authenticate through IAS (either against the IAS password store, or forwarded by IAS to your corporate IdP) [1]. Note that two populations are already IAS-only ahead of everyone else: new hires (onboardees) and external learners can only log in through IAS [5]. So if onboarding or external learning is live, part of this is effectively already in force for you.

2. Third-party corporate IdP direct integration

Your corporate identity provider (Entra ID / Azure AD, Okta, Ping, OneLogin, Google Workspace) configured as a SAML asserting party directly against SuccessFactors, with no SAP identity layer in between.

The word doing all the work is direct. You are not losing your corporate IdP. You are losing the ability to point it straight at SuccessFactors "without SAP Identity Services as proxy" [1]. The trust that currently terminates at SF must instead terminate at IAS, which then fronts SF. Your users keep authenticating against Entra/Okta/Ping exactly as before: the direct SF-to-IdP trust is what's removed [1][2].

What breaks: the direct SAML trust stops working, and users routed through it can't authenticate, unless that same IdP has been re-established as a corporate IdP inside IAS first.

3. Support for multiple asserting parties (direct)

A specific case of #2 worth calling out because larger orgs hit it: if you currently run more than one corporate IdP as multiple SAML asserting parties directly against SuccessFactors (e.g., different regions or business units, with a chooser screen or default asserting party), support for multiple asserting parties is deprecated by this date [4]. The replacement is to configure those same multiple IdPs as multiple corporate identity providers within IAS, with IAS doing the routing [4].

What breaks: the multi-IdP selection at the SF layer goes away; if you haven't rebuilt that routing in IAS, users behind the secondary asserting parties lose their path in.

4. Partial Organization SSO

The side-by-side pattern where one group of users authenticates through a corporate IdP while another group still logs in with a SuccessFactors username and password. Partial Organization SSO reached end of maintenance May 16, 2025 and is deleted with the 2H 2026 release [3].

What breaks: the side-by-side mechanism at the SF layer is removed. The capability itself, routing different populations to different authenticators, is rebuilt inside IAS as conditional authentication (KBA 2954556) [3][13]. So this isn't "you lose partial SSO forever," it's "partial SSO moves into IAS and you have to configure it there."

What "deleted" concretely means

Not "deprecated but tolerated." Deleted. SAP's language is unambiguous: after the deprecation completes, customers can no longer access SAP SuccessFactors with basic authentication or third-party corporate IdP direct integration [1][12]. In practice:

  • Basic-auth users and direct-IdP users who have not moved to IAS cannot authenticate: this is a login outage for those populations, not a warning banner [1].
  • New hires (onboardees) and external learners are already IAS-only, so unmigrated onboarding is the population that breaks first, not last [5].
  • IAS itself remains the answer for everyone: password users authenticate against IAS, and corporate-IdP users are forwarded by IAS to their IdP [4]. Everything ends up behind IAS.

Why SAP is doing this

Worth a paragraph, because it frames the migration as more than compliance homework [12]:

  • Basic authentication is simply weaker. It offers less security than the methods common to modern cloud applications: there's no additional mechanism to verify the user, and passwords are routinely reused across accounts and apps, which raises the risk of compromise. Moving to IAS is what unlocks multi-factor and risk-based authentication for SuccessFactors [12].
  • One identity platform instead of many point integrations. Putting IAS in front (as main or proxy IdP) unifies the login experience across all SAP applications and lets SAP harmonize UI and API authentication on the same platform, which in turn makes authorization management cleaner. It also cuts maintenance, because IAS ships pre-built integration and provisioning to SuccessFactors and other SAP products, rather than you hand-maintaining a direct IdP-to-SF trust [12].

And the flip side, if you don't migrate [12]:

  1. You can't adopt current security and compliance baselines such as MFA for cloud applications.
  2. You're left on an old, unsupported authentication architecture that is itself slated for deprecation.
  3. After the deprecation completes, you lose access via basic auth or direct-IdP entirely.

What is NOT going away with the 2H 2026 release (the confusion-killers)

This is where most planning goes wrong. Several things sound like they belong to this release and don't. Pin these down so nobody on your team conflates them.

 

API Basic Authentication (the OData/SOAP auth method)November 12, 2027 (phased)A separate deprecation on the API side. End of maintenance was June 2, 2023; deletion is November 12, 2027, with Basic Auth off by default in 1H 2027 (opt-in for migration) and fully disabled in 2H 2027. Don't let this later date lull you: login is November 13, 2026, and the EC items below die even earlier [6].
Login using SuccessFactors employment usernameMay 15, 2026 (earlier)A narrower login variant, deprecated ahead of the main date. After it, users can still log in via IAS (login name + password) or via basic auth using account login name until the login deprecation; employment-username logins must switch to account login name [5].
SOAP-based Employee Central SFAPIs (except CompoundEmployee), plus Basic Auth for EC integration connectivity (core hybrid / payroll, ref. ECT-263763)November 20, 2026API/integration items that die a year before general API Basic Auth (Nov 2027). If you run SOAP SFAPIs or core-hybrid/payroll integrations, this earlier date governs them, not the 2027 one [8].
SSO signing certificateAlready expired June 2, 2025Not a future date at all: it's already gone. If you're still direct, this already bit you [9].
Report Center SFTP report deliveryNot affectedUses the platform's internal scheduling framework, authenticated separately. No change required, a common false alarm (KBA 3724884) [7].

The one-line takeaway: login and API are separate tracks with different dates. Login/UI Basic Auth lands with the 2H 2026 release (Preview October 12, Production November 13, 2026). General API Basic Auth runs to November 12, 2027 (off by default in 1H 2027, fully disabled in 2H 2027); but SOAP SFAPIs and EC integration connectivity die a year earlier, on November 20, 2026, so 2027 is not blanket breathing room [1][6][8][12].

How to tell if you're exposed

Concrete self-checks, not a project: do these first.

  • Basic Auth (UI): if the SuccessFactors default username/password login page appears and a login succeeds, you have basic-auth users. If users land on the IAS login page or are redirected to their corporate IdP, they're already behind IAS [3].
  • Direct corporate IdP: in Provisioning under Single Sign-On (SSO) Settings, check your SAML asserting parties. A corporate IdP configured as an enabled asserting party directly (with IAS not the enabled asserting party) is a direct integration that must move [4].
  • Multiple asserting parties: more than one enabled asserting party (or a user-facing "choose your IdP" screen) means you're relying on the multi-asserting-party support that's being removed [4].
  • Partial Organization SSO: same test as Basic Auth: if some users hit the default SF username/password page and log in successfully while others are redirected, Partial Org SSO is still enabled and you're exposed [3].

If every population already lands on IAS or a corporate IdP (never the native SF login page), you may be further along than you think: IAS has been auto-deployed for tenants created after December 9, 2022 (KBA 2791410) [12].

What to do about it (the short version)

The fix for all four items is the same destination: move authentication onto IAS. The process runs through the Upgrade Center: run "Initiate the SAP Cloud Identity Services Identity Authentication Service Integration," configure IAS/IPS and your corporate-IdP trust (and rebuild multi-IdP routing and conditional/partial authentication inside IAS via KBA 2954556), then run "Activate SuccessFactors Identity Authentication Service Integration" (KBA 2791410) [12][13]. Activation is a one-way door and forces a successful test first, so do it in Preview before Production [12].

The reason to start now, not in Q4: SAP is running the Initiate (and then Activate) tasks for tenants not yet on IAS, on a mass-enablement schedule between July and September 2026, in support of the 2H 2026 release [11]. That keeps your users' access continuous, but an automated Activate is an uncontrolled cutover: no testing window you designed, no conditional-auth rules you built, no dry run. Running Initiate yourself, ahead of the automation, is how you keep control of the configuration and give yourself runway to validate before the release [11]. IAS adoption is mandatory by November 2026 regardless (KBA 3097769) [10].

Layer the Preview-first trap on top of this: with Preview deletion on October 12, your practical window to have Preview migrated and testable is even shorter than the November headline suggests.

Resources & getting help

SAP has put real enablement behind this migration. Use it [12]:

  • Announcement blog: Deprecation of Basic Auth / 3rd Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SuccessFactors.
  • Migration community blog: Migration to SAP Cloud Identity Authentication (IAS/IPS), with resources and how-to videos.
  • Mass-enablement blog: Mass enablement of Initiate IAS Upgrade and Activate IAS tasks to enforce…, the authoritative source for the July–September 2026 automation [11].
  • Help Portal: Migration to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service, and the release-note page for this deprecation [2].
  • Step-by-Step Guide of SF to IAS Integration Process and Common Troubleshooting Tips: SAP Community (search the title; a practical walkthrough with troubleshooting).
  • SAP Discovery Center: Mission: Learn to integrate HCM with SAP Cloud Identity Services in SAP SuccessFactors and migrate to IAS/IPS (SAP has moved the IAS/IPS e-learning here).
  • IAS/IPS Open Office Hours: now held once monthly via the SAP Learning Hub (a valid S-user and one-time Enterprise Support Academy sign-up are required); registration is linked from the migration community blog above.
  • If something breaks during migration, open an incident under component LOD-SF-PLT-IAS (Identity Authentication Services with BizX) [1][12].
  • For onboarding-specific IAS issues, use LOD-SF-OBX-IAS;
  • For IAS product issues, BC-IAM-IDS;
  • For IPS configuration, BC-IAM-IPS.

References

Numbered in order of first appearance. Every SAP KBA below is hyperlinked to its public SAP support page. Verify live dates on the SAP Help Portal before finalizing any plan. SAP has revised adjacent deadlines before, and the two-wave release timing (Preview October 12 / Production November 13, 2026) is exactly the kind of thing worth re-checking against the live page.

[1] SAP KBA 3472405: Deprecation of Basic Authentication and Third-Party Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite. End of maintenance June 2, 2025; deletion with the 2H 2026 release; "no direct integration to SAP SuccessFactors after deprecation." Component LOD-SF-PLT-IAS.

[2] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Basic Authentication and Third-Party Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite (What's New / release information page).

[3] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Partial Organization SSO in SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite. End of maintenance May 16, 2025; deleted November 13, 2026 (body text contains a stray "November 15, 2026"); includes the default-login-page detection test.

[4] SAP: Setting Up SAP SuccessFactors with Identity Authentication (integration admin guide). Source for IAS as real vs proxy IdP, asserting-party selection behavior, and "support for multiple asserting parties will also be deprecated by November 2026."

[5] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of User Login Using Employment Username. Employment-username login deprecated by May 15, 2026; basic auth deprecated by November 13, 2026; new hires (onboardees) and external learners are IAS-only.

[6] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Basic Authentication for APIs (the API side; deletion revised to November 12, 2027). See the API companion guide.

[7] SAP KBA 3724884: Potential impacts of Basic Authentication Deprecation for Report Center SFTP schedules (SFTP report delivery not affected).

[8] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Employee Central SFAPIs Except CompoundEmployee (deletion November 20, 2026 as publicly confirmed).

[9] SAP Help Portal: Renewal of SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite Single Sign-On Certificate (certificate expired June 2, 2025).

[10] SAP KBA 3097769: IAS / IPS – Is IAS implementation mandatory for all SuccessFactors customers? (mandatory by November 2026).

[11] SAP Community (Product and Customer Updates): Mass enablement of Initiate IAS Upgrade and Activate IAS tasks to enforce…, plus SAP's direct customer notice. Source for the July–September 2026 mass-enablement window and the automatic Initiate/Activate behavior.

[12] SAP KBA 2791410: Integrating SuccessFactors with Identity Authentication (IAS) through the Upgrade Center, together with SAP's customer communication for this deprecation. Source for the Initiate → configure → Activate process, IAS auto-deployment for tenants after December 9, 2022, the two-wave deletion dates (Preview October 12 / Production November 13, 2026), SAP's rationale, and the resource list.

[13] SAP KBA 2954556: How to implement Partial SSO after Identity Authentication (IAS) upgrade on SuccessFactors (the conditional-authentication replacement for Partial Organization SSO).

entry.title: Q2 2026 Innovations: SAP Enterprise Service Management
entry.content:
Q2 went by fast! I hope that you all were able to participate in SAPPHIRE 2026 either in person or virtually. The SAP Autonomous Enterprise was announced and a shout out of huge thanks to our service customers Pfizer and Doehler for sharing your incredible stories & insights about your transformation, AI adoption and value outcomes with SAP Service Cloud and SAP ESM! Our next value-add event is queued up -  SAP Connect 2026 is coming to Las Vegas, October 5-7 and we welcome our customers and partners to secure an early bird rate by August 10th to discover how to maximize value of your solutions, explore best practices in AI and business transformation approaches and much, much more!

Before I jump into our Q2 innovations for SAP ESM there are a couple of exciting opportunities I’d like to share.

  • It’s not too late to join our SAP ESM webcast on Tuesday, July 21st, focused on HR Service Delivery: AI, Data, and Experience: From Transactions to Intelligent HR Services also featuring a widely recognized expert Anita Lettink. If you’re reading post-event, click the link above and watch the replay!
  • Also, there is a new SAP Learning module for SAP ESM: Integrating SAP Enterprise Service Management. This course is available at no cost and is designed specifically for administrators, consultants, and business users to deepen their knowledge of SAP Enterprise Service Management. This course focuses on integration configuration to enable execution of end-to-end scenarios delivering true value and operational efficiency across the organization.

Now back to the focus of this blog – our Q2 innovations for SAP ESM! There are so many innovations and improvements please register for our Q2 Innovation webcast July 29th hosted by our product team.

Self-Service Catalog

The Self-Service Catalog provides employees with a guided, intuitive way to find and request the services they need, without requiring an understanding of the underlying processes or systems. Requests submitted through the appropriate topic catalog or tile are automatically resolved or routed based on the inquiry context, which reduces manual triage effort, frustration in solutioning and shortens resolution times. For service agents, this means fewer incomplete or misrouted cases and more time spent on higher-value work. For employees, it means a simpler, more predictable experience when engaging with their enterprise or shared service teams.

Service Catalog configuration

                                                                                             

Service Catalog Self Service

 

Content Package Framework

The Content Package Framework enables organizations to deploy SAP ESM more quickly by leveraging prebuilt, reusable configurations for case types, workflows, and catalogs. Rather than building service processes from the ground up, customers and SI partners can start from proven templates and adapt them to specific business needs. This reduces implementation effort, simplifies rollout across lines of business, and helps organizations standardize service delivery at scale. Content packages for HR service delivery are planned for a future release.

New Email Editor

The new email editor provides service agents with a modern, streamlined interface for composing, editing, and managing customer communications. Designed with an AI-first architecture in mind, the editor supports faster email creation while maintaining the quality and consistency expected in service interactions. Coupled with the AI email drafter capability, agents truly spend less time formatting and more time focused on resolving customer issues, which improves response times and communication quality.

 

Email editor

Creation of Sales Objects from the Customer Hub

While this capability is more closely aligned with SAP Service Cloud, sales support and B2B supplier support are common use cases we see deployed for SAP ESM as well. With that said, service agents can now create and manage leads, opportunities, appointments, and sales orders directly from the service agent desktop when the interaction calls for it, reducing clicks and upskilling time. The capability supports closer alignment between service and sales teams and delivers that seamless experience you expect between SAP solutions.

 Self-Service Enhancements

Two enhancements provide administrators with greater control over the self-service experience:

  • Edit Form in Self-Service During Case Lifecycle feature allows administrators to define conditions under which a form remains editable, giving employees the ability to update information at appropriate points in the case lifecycle without agent involvement.
  • Adapt Case Details Screen per Case Type feature allows administrators to configure the Case Details screen in Self-Service on a per-case-type basis, ensuring that users only see fields and actions that are relevant to their specific scenario.

Together, these enhancements help drive higher self-service adoption and reduce unnecessary agent handoffs.

Embedded Component for Custom Services

Administrators can now define list views or object forms within a custom service and embed them into custom user interfaces, as well as selected standard UIs. By mapping parameters from the host page, related custom records can be viewed and worked on directly within the relevant UI view. This capability makes it easier to extend SAP ESM to fit unique business processes and ensures that agents and users have access to contextual information exactly where they need it, without navigating away from their current task.

New Self-Service Experience (Restricted Release)

A new Self-Service experience for SAP ESM is being introduced as a restricted release. Aligned with the latest self-service catalog capabilities, the new experience is designed to support cross-functional deployment across the enterprise, providing instant solutions to common inquiries and enabling contextual case creation and management. As this is a restricted release, customers interested in exploring the capability are encouraged to request access via the solution. Early feedback from customers will play an important role in shaping the general availability release, and participation is strongly encouraged.

Sign In as Another User

Administrators can now sign in as another business user seeing the same functionality and authorizations as that user. This allows administrators to reproduce and troubleshoot issues from the user’s perspective without screen sharing or password exchange. This results in faster issue resolution, stronger security practices, and a more efficient support process for both administrators and end users.

Analytics Improvements

This release delivers two important improvements to analytics capabilities:

  • Embedded SAC Widgets allow SAP Analytics Cloud visualizations to be embedded directly into the SAP ESM interface, displaying focused analytics with individual tiles within the daily flow of various business objects such as cases, customers and opportunities. These widgets can also be incorporated into mashups for more comprehensive analytics overall.
  • Custom Service Entities in Analytics allow custom service entities to be used as data sources in SAP Analytics Cloud. Once a custom entity is marked as Analytics Relevant, the system automatically generates a corresponding data source that appears under Custom Data Sources in the Query Designer, where it can be combined with standard data sources to produce tailored analytics content.

These enhancements bring insights closer to where work happens and reduce the need to switch between systems for data-driven decision-making.

Approval Process Simplification

Two enhancements to approval processes help organizations improve consistency and reduce delays.

  • Configure Approval and Rejection Reasons: Administrators can now configure predefined approval and rejection reasons, create custom reason lists, assign them to specific approval processes, and control whether reason selection is mandatory or optional. This supports more consistent decision-making and improves auditability across approval workflows.
  • Automatic Approval Settings: Configuration allowing approval steps to be automatically approved or rejected if no action is taken within a defined duration can be defined from one hour to ten days. This helps ensure that workflows continue to progress even when approvers are unavailable, reducing bottlenecks and shortening overall case cycle times.

Accelerating Case Resolution with UX Simplicity and Automation

Several enhancements in this release focus on simplifying the case experience and giving administrators more configuration control. A few examples include:

  • Case Data Retention Rules by Case Type: This feature allows administrators to configure retention rules for automated transaction depersonalization on a per-case-type basis, helping the organization meet legal, regional, and business-specific data retention requirements.
  • Multiple Catalogs Mapped to One Case Type: The ability to map multiple catalogs to one case type allows administrators to assign additional category catalogs to a case type, minimizing configuration effort where similar case types can be used across various business functions.
  • Hide Category Levels in Case: This feature allows administrators to selectively hide specific category levels within cases, providing agents with a cleaner and more focused interface by removing unnecessary hierarchical information.

Case adaptation

These enhancements help streamline the agent experience while giving administrators the flexibility to align case management with compliance, business, and operational requirements.

Useful Information

For additional Q2 innovations relevant for SAP Enterprise Service Management please reference Kirsi Tarvainen’s blog on SAP Service Cloud and register for the upcoming Q2 Release Webinar. To stay up-to-date as we release capabilities each month and to get a full view of what’s new for Q2, reference the What’s New page in our help portal. Also, reference these valuable links for more on SAP ESM and SAP CX solutions:

SAP CX Q2 2020 Innovation News

SAP Learning ESM Course

SAP Service Cloud V2 Certification Course

SAP Release Navigator

SAP ESM’s free trial Get hands-on experience today with an HR service delivery scenario (Finance shared services coming soon).

Use the SAP Enterprise Service Management managed tag when posting on SAP ESM topics in the community!

And finally, thank you. The continued growth of this community, and the willingness of its members to share, question, and collaborate, is what moves SAP ESM forward. Every SAP employee, partner, and customer benefits from the collective knowledge exchanged here and it’s genuinely appreciated.

entry.title: Employee Central Data Migration: Notes from the Field, Alongside SAP's Standard Guide
entry.content:
I have been working on the SAP SF Employee Central module for the last ten years, and in every implementation project I have done, there is always an Employee Master Data migration. The Standard SAP SF Data Migration guide (IDP) is thorough, but at least six data sets demand more field judgment than any guide alone can give. Think of this blog as the practical companion to that guide, not a replacement, from a consultant who has run these templates more times than I can count.

Check Point:-

Align with the client on Employee ID range and sequence before starting upload, especially on multi-country implementations creating new SF IDs for legacy employees. Get this agreed first; once an ID is created, it is very difficult to purge and recreate.

 

1) Basic Import (UDF)

The first template to upload, and where consultants waste the most time filling every column, mandatory or not.

 

Recommendation 1: Per the standard guide, you only need Status, User ID, Username, First Name, and Last Name. Fields like Manager ID, HR ID, Title, phone, location, and email get updated automatically from later templates via HRIS Sync, so omit them here; the Sync run overrides this data anyway even if you do fill it in.

 

Exception: if EC is going live into a landscape where other SuccessFactors modules are already active, do not load the UDF first, use it only for employees hired after the last UDF import, and skip Manager/HR fields if modules like Performance already have live forms running, an incorrect manager can shift those forms unnecessarily.

 

2) Position

Major concerns I have always seen when creating position data for migration.

a ) What is the right date for creating positions?

 

Recommendation 2: Positions can practically be created with any backdated date, e.g. 01/01/1900 or 01/01/2000, based on the age of the legacy data. My preference is to date position creation similar to the employee's hire date (regular position, FTE 1), which keeps the position's “age” accurate for later reporting.

 

b) How do you map the manager's position at creation?

 

Recommendation 3: I'd suggest not mapping it at creation, since your current manager may not have been the manager at hire. Instead, map it at your cutoff date (e.g. 1st Jul 26) as a second position entry.

The standard guide also documents a configuration-driven alternative, using Position Management settings (Adapt Hierarchy During Position Import, Adapt Hierarchy in Job Info Import) so the system assigns the correct manager automatically. Either works; I prefer the manual route for the added control.

 

Recommendation 4: Be cautious of cyclic dependency when mapping manager ID. E.g. if A reports to B, and B reports to C, there is no way C can end up reporting to A.

 

c)How do you upload position data with minimum effort?

 

Recommendation 5: Reverse engineer instead of iterating. Since Position is an MDF object, create a few via the UI with different field/picklist combinations, export them, and your template with sample data is ready.

 

3) Job Information

The most difficult template of employee data migration.

 

Recommendation 6: Align with the customer to upload only two records, Hire and Go-Live, pushing remaining history into Internal Job History instead.

One caveat: if a Compensation Merit Cycle or Variable Pay is in scope for the go-live year, the customer needs at least one year of Job, Compensation, and Personal Information history; two records alone won't support the merit process.

 

Recommendation 7: The Hire record should carry NO_MANAGER, for the same reason as position creation, with minimal fields: ID, event reason, event date, legal entity, and position number. Add legacy org/job data only if the customer already has it in correct format.

 

Recommendation 8: The Go-Live/Latest record is where you should target filling all fields accurately, including Manager ID, grades, title, and org information.

Make sure Legal Entity, Business Unit, Division, Department, etc. carry the correct codes with correct association, wrong association is one of the most common causes of errors, even when codes themselves are right.

 

Recommendation 9: Turn off Workflow, Event Derivation, and Cross Portlet Rules on Job/Compensation during migration, these trigger unwanted updates on import.

Rules that simply default Job/Org attributes from Position can stay on if it aids data quality, provided each batch stays under about two hours.

 

 

4) Dependents

Second most complex object after Job Information. Here is how we can crack this nut.

 

Recommendation 10: Create a unique Dependent ID by concatenating Employee ID with a dependent sequence, e.g. employee 10000000 with four dependents gets 1000000001 through 1000000004.

Upload these with DOB under Biographical Information first, then Personal Information, National ID, etc. sequentially.

Each dependent needs at least one non-effective-dated record for the setup to be valid.

Split the template into smaller pieces and upload separately.

 

5) Recurring Deductions

Unique among SF objects, being a parent-child object.

 

Recommendation 11: First create deduction data using the Recurring Deduction template with User ID and effective date. Once this parent object is uploaded, then upload the child, Recurring Deduction Items.

Splitting the problem this way makes it far easier; a combined deduction file will keep failing and leave you exhausted.

Reverse engineer here too: map deductions for a few employees via the UI, export them, and use that to understand how end dates should be structured.

 

6) Bank/Payment Information

A similar parent-child pattern. Bank data is the parent, employee Bank Details the child.

 

Recommendation 12: Create Bank data first, with country, routing number, and Business Identifier Code as the foundation. Then upload employee Bank Details with a hire date matching the routing number, BIC, and payment method as Bank Transfer, otherwise it will keep failing. Same advice: reverse engineer bank details too.

 

Conclusion:-

There can be other challenging issues with data upload, but based on my experience, complex objects like Position, Job Information, Dependents, and Bank/Deductions can mostly be solved with 1) the recommendations above, 2) breaking templates into smaller pieces, and 3) reverse engineering.

This is what I have learnt so far, alongside the official guide, not instead of it. What data points do you find challenging, and what has worked for you?

Thanks for reading this article!

Mohammad Saddam Aquib

entry.title: Diagnostic Tool (Employee Data Changes)
entry.content:
You can also check 🔗️Link to the Article Diagnostic Tool (Workflow) :  Diagnostic Tool (Workflow)

🔍What is a Diagnostic Tool: It is a dedicated troubleshooting tool for issues with HRIS elements for both workflow approvals and data transactions happened through “Workflows” & “Centralised services”, that triggered either Manager Self Service (or) Employee Self Service” for both ON SAVE and On Change event types. Instead of getting lost in the Rule Trace excel execution logs cell by cell from its execution logs, this tool enables the admins to have the upper hand by having an enabled view of Snapshots on employee data changes and the business rules which are all triggered and which are all involved on the data changes happened in the system and with it’s corresponding cascading changes (dependent HRIS fields) happened in the other areas of the systems of current and future dated records. Data from the Trace would be retained only for 10 days in the Diagnostic tool, then purged after 10 days.

Prerequisite RBP enablement required to successfully access the Diagnostic tool and it’s allied features.

You have these permissions:

1 🚩

  • Administrator > Diagnostic Tool >Diagnostics Tracing Configuration
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Workflows Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Centralised Services Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Admin Centre Permissions >Access Diagnostic Tool

2 🚩

Now you have to configure the Diagnostics Trace Configuration: diagnosticTrace object, and its details are defined in Manage Data, and it will be actively spanned for 15 minutes to capture the data changes that happened through Centralised services.

Type of transaction supported for the data trace:

 1️⃣Workflows    2️⃣Employee Data Changes

Let’s simulate the transaction in the Diagnostic tool ->Employee Data Changes”.

As a prerequisite, you need to enable / setup the “Diagnostic Trace Configuration” in the Manage data (as you do it for Rule Trace 🔍 ). While setting up the trace object, the following are important attributes you can define and narrow down your search details based on your business scenarios. You can set the following attributes –

⭐Prime Attribute – Set the trace config status to Active

👉Start Date:

👉Requested By & For: The system start to perform the active trace for the employee data changes by the defined attributes of “Requested By” & “Requested For” in the diagnostic trace config object. If you forget to define this information, the system will not initiate the data trace.

Business Action: This field is non-mandatory. If you want, you can also skip this field, also you see this field column in your diagnostic search result as well. No need to have big thinking 😎, it gives you the course of action info, what user action made the system to trigger the source data changes either with the values either (MSS (or) ESS (or) Import, etc)

👉Additional Info:

*Supported Business Rule Scenarios: ON SAVE & ON CHANGE [HRIS Elements]

*Track centralised services for imports & API’s if the record is less than 10.

*Diagnostic tool captures execution details of ON SAVE rules during data imports up to  10 records of each import.

[Employee Data Changes] transaction trace details are always presented by two unique views (Purposely).  Snapshot view - Foundation of Data Changes / Rule Execution View - Business Rule is the foundation of the data, what are all involved in the data changes.

1 Snapshot View : Tabular record details for each stage of a save transaction. It showcases the entity that underwent the data changes by the user action either through (ONSAVE / ON CHANGE) event rules. It holds the HRIS field values, which got updated with features of striking out the old values, and colour code (identifier) of the newly added values. Green code of Added / Update. RED coded of Deleted values.

Snapshot data is nested with the transaction stages for an overview of data, so that you will have a clear understanding of the data transaction happened in the system

1️⃣Data Changes (1): Shows the record of which HRIS entity field underwent the value change by user. The number indicates the number of HRIS entities that were originally changed by the user and the corresponding changes performed by the dedicated business rule in place (Numeric count provides additional information on HRIS entities that underwent value corrections).

️Simulated Example: User has performed the changes only on the HRIS field – Location. (1- Job Info - Location)

2️⃣Related Changes (1): Shows the other field (or) data changes due to the source field value changes (originally changed as part of the data changes through ONSAVE (or) ONCHANGE events).

3️⃣Rules(4): Shows the details of the business rules executed with the number of records within the entity (or) with cross-entity as well. The Snapshot View doesn't show rule names. You can navigate to Rule Execution View to know about the rule details, which are all executed. Only changes triggered by onSave rules and onChange rules are traceable. A navigation button to “Rule Execution View” is provided within this section.

Simulated Example: Here, I have used a cross-entity rule, where a change of Location in Job Information will update the expected business values of the Pay Group field in the Comp Info category of the user profile.

💡- Snapshot view provides only the field-level information changes on the source field and other cascading fields through (ONSAVE / ONCHANGE) business rules.

Rules (1-Job Info Record): Number of rules executed and performed the data and cascading data changes on its respective HRIS entities. In this example, the change of location updated the Event Reason via an event derivation rule in Job Information.

Rule (1 – Comp Info): Cross-entity rule updated the comp field Pay Group

Rule (2  Pay Comp Recurring): Existing 2 pay components have moved to the new effective date from the previous stack records. Where you can see the updated event date.

4️⃣End-Date Correction (8):  This stage shows the changes to the end date field values relative to the record being updated or added. Use this to check if there's an end date issue during the transactions. Highly focused on the End Date Value.

Shows the end date correction happened on the entity level; the current existing active record gets delimited, and a new effective-dated record has been inserted.

5️⃣Forward Propagation (8) : Able to see, what are all the fields and it’s values that has been forward propagated as part of the data correction/update.

2.Rule Execution View : Supports both event types of  OnSave & OnChange rules, which trigger the employee data changes.

Rule Execution View provides details on the rules which triggered and performed the data update / Creation / Deletion in both Source and  Target HRIS entities.

You can able to see the quick Snapshot details in the rule execution view, and also you can able to navigate to the rule definition (Config Buss Rules) from embedded button provided.

My Other Published Links:

1.Read Audit Report Configuration

2. Creation of Deep Links in SuccessFactors Suites

 

🙏Thank You

Thank you for taking the time to read this article.

I have invested significant time and effort in preparing this content with the goal of helping SAP SuccessFactors professionals better understand and utilize the Diagnostic Tool. My intention has been to present the information in a concise and easy-to-understand manner, supported by practical scenarios and straightforward explanations.

entry.title: Diagnostic Tool (Workflow)
entry.content:
You can also check the 🔗 Link to the Article Diagnostic Tool (Employee Data Changes) : Diagnostic Tool (Employee Data Changes)

Introduction:

What is a Diagnostic Tool: It is a dedicated troubleshooting tool for issues with HRIS elements for both workflow approvals and data transactions happened through “Workflows” & “Centralised services”, that triggered either Manager Self Service (or) Employee Self Service” for both ON S AVE and On Change event types. Instead of getting lost in the Rule Trace excel execution logs cell by cell from its execution logs, this tool enables the admins to have the upper hand by having an enabled view of Snapshots on employee data changes and the business rules which are all triggered and which are all involved on the data changes happened in the system and with it’s corresponding cascading changes (dependent HRIS fields) happened in the other areas of the systems of current and future dated records. Data from the Trace would be retained only for 10 days in the Diagnostic tool, then purged after 10 days.

Prerequisite RBP enablement required to successfully access the Diagnostic tool and it’s allied features.

Main Portion:

You need to have these role-based permissions to successfully access the diagnostic tools

1 🚩

  • Administrator  >Diagnostic Tool > Diagnostics Tracing Configuration
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Workflows Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Centralised Services Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Admin Centre Permissions >Access Diagnostic Tool

Type of transaction supported for the data trace:

 1️⃣Workflows    2️⃣Employee Data Changes

->Let’s simulate the first type of transaction supported in the Diagnostic tool's  ->“Workflows”.

Only RBP enablement is required for the workflow transaction. No need to set up MDF trace object for this transaction. From day 1 of RBP enablement in the tenant, all the HRIS-enabled workflows are under the radar of the diagnostic tool.

🖋️Taking an example of a very simple business rule: obtaining approval from the first-level manager for a username change.

Diagnostic Tool: Input the required details to pull the information from the system.

🧗First level of information pulled (Overview and quick brief of the details for the triggered workflow)

Further navigating to the next level of information: You can see all holistic information of the workflow status, and the relevant information on which entity the business rule has been placed and the details of workflow configuration and Workflow business rule and workflow id would be seen here (All displayed hyperlinked values; you can navigate to its detailed information directly by clicking on it)

You need to remember following features and limitations:

  • Auto approvals on your workflow config setup is also supported (if you enabled in your instance)
  • Workflows based on HRIS elements are supported and not supported for MDF objects until (Version #2605)
  • Workflow diagnostic data captures the Workflow transaction only if the approvals  are done through the Workflow Details page. Approvals are done through other way around through quick actions (or) through centralised tool "Manage Workflow Request" tool are not traceable of the workflow troubleshooting.
  • Workflows done through Sf Mobile Application are also not supported.

Take Away: When there is business requirement on amending the existing complex business rules especially with the cross-entity rules (or) new workflow configurations (or) troubleshooting the reported issues on your existing workflow approval not being received by the approver, you can simulate and troubleshoot yourself and see what is happening at the back end of the system configuration.

 

Thanks Note:

🙏Thank You

Thanks for taking your time for reading this article. Hope this article would have served additional insight on the potential troubleshooting tool called - Diagnostic Tool.

entry.title: Job Classification (EC) vs. Job Role (JPB): Two Objects You Have to Keep in Sync Yourself
entry.content:
Introduction

A question that comes up in almost every design workshop where Employee Central (EC) and Job Profile Builder (JPB) are both in scope: we already have Job Classifications in EC, so why do we need Job Roles in JPB? They sound interchangeable, and teams often assume maintaining one takes care of the other. It doesn't. Here's what each object is for, how they connect, and why a simplified 1:1 job architecture still means double maintenance.

The two objects and what they do

Job Classification is a Foundation Object in Employee Central. It's the administrative side of the job - assigned to positions and to Job Information, carrying attributes like pay grade, employee class and standard hours that drive business rules, reporting and integrations.

Job Role belongs to Job Profile Builder (Families and Roles). It's the talent side. It holds the job profile content: description, responsibilities, competencies and skills. This is what Recruiting, Performance, Succession and Career Development actually consume.

How they connect

The two are linked through a mapping maintained in Manage Job Profile Content, or via the Role–Job Code import template for bulk changes. The chain is simple: an employee holds a Job Classification in Job Information, the classification is mapped to a Role, and through the Role the employee gets their job profile.

Why the simplified design doesn't remove the effort

Many of us choose a simplified architecture: one Job Classification = one Job Role, same name, same code. Clean and easy to explain. But there is no automatic sync between the two objects. Creating a Job Classification does not create a Job Role, and renaming one does not rename the other.

Every job catalog change is therefore a three-step exercise: update the Job Classification in EC, update the Job Role in JPB, and maintain the Role-to-Classification mapping. Miss a step and nothing errors out - things just quietly break. An employee with no job profile, competencies missing from a performance form, a requisition pulling stale content.

What has worked for me: one owner and one intake process for catalog changes covering both objects, aligned external codes from day one, imports for mass changes, and a periodic reconciliation of active classifications against mapped roles.

Conclusion

Job Classification and Job Role answer two different questions: "what is this person administratively assigned to?" vs. "what does this job require?" SuccessFactors keeps them as independent objects with only a manually maintained mapping between them. A 1:1 simplified architecture is still a sound choice; it just doesn't eliminate the dual maintenance, so plan your governance around it rather than discovering the drift later.

entry.title: Brains Meet Boundaries: Business Rules and Agentic AI in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
For years, business rules have been the backbone of automation in SAP SuccessFactors. They ensure HR processes remain consistent, compliant, and aligned with organizational policies. Whether it's validating employee data, determining recruiting template or new hire onboarding program, leave eligibility, or routing approvals, business rules have successfully automated countless HR transactions within SuccessFactors. However, the rise of Agentic AI is changing the conversation. Instead of asking, "What happens when a condition is met?", administrators are beginning to ask, "How can an AI agent achieve an HR objective while respecting our policies and governance?". This shift doesn't replace business rules, rather it elevates their role. Today, what we see is Business rules becoming the guardrails that define organizational boundaries while on the other hand, AI agents becoming the intelligent collaborators capable of orchestrating complete business outcomes. Using an objective transition from Rule-Based Automation to Goal-Based Execution, a few use cases are described below:

Business Rule: If

  • {Performance Rating ≥ 4} and {Years of Service ≥ 2}
    Then
  • {Employee is eligible for promotion}

The rule executes exactly as designed. It neither questions nor expands the decision.

AI Agent: Approaches the same scenario differently:

    • Review historical performance trends.
    • Analyze the employee's skills and certifications.
    • Evaluate succession plans.
    • Compare internal pay equity.
    • Check departmental budgets.
    • Prepare the promotion justification.
    • Route approvals.
    • Recommend leadership learning after promotion.

Output: The original business rule still determines eligibility. The AI agent simply manages everything around it to deliver the desired business outcome. This represents one of the most significant shifts in enterprise HR automation.

 

Business Rules Become Governance, Not Workflow - As organizations adopt Agentic AI, business rules evolve from being workflow drivers to becoming governance mechanisms. Instead of controlling every step of a process, they define what an AI agent is allowed to do.

Use Case 1: Hiring Process.

Traditional SuccessFactors Process

  • Validate mandatory employee information.
  • Check duplicate employee records.
  • Assign onboarding tasks.
  • Route approvals.

Agentic AI-Enabled Process
The hiring manager simply says: "Hire Priya as a Senior Consultant starting August 1."
The AI agent:

    • Collects missing information from the hiring manager.
    • Validates mandatory business rules.
    • Recommends the appropriate job classification.
    • Creates onboarding activities.
    • Coordinates IT equipment requests.
    • Books induction sessions.
    • Notifies payroll and facilities.
    • Monitors completion of onboarding tasks.

Output: You will observe that throughout the process, the AI agent never bypasses business rules. Instead, it orchestrates multiple systems while remaining compliant with organizational policies.

 

Use Case 2: Leave Management

Traditional Business Rule: {If leave balance is sufficient, approve the request.}

Agentic AI: {The AI agent evaluates}:

  • Team availability.
  • Project deadlines.
  • Public holidays.
  • Historical leave patterns.
  • Manager preferences.

Output: If the requested dates conflict with a critical project, the AI agent might recommend alternative dates that satisfy both employee preferences and business needs, while explaining the reasoning to the employee and manager.

 

Use Case 3: Internal Mobility

While business rules validate employee eligibility before applying for an internal role in recruiting, Agentic AI extends this capability for recruiters: Instead of waiting for employees to search for opportunities, an AI agent can proactively identify individuals whose skills align with upcoming vacancies within the system.

Example: An employee completes multiple cloud certifications.

The AI agent:

  • Detects the new qualifications.
  • Identifies suitable open positions.
  • Recommends additional learning to close remaining skill gaps.
  • Notifies the employee about relevant opportunities.
  • Informs HR of potential internal candidates.

Output: The business rules still determine eligibility, but the AI agent creates a far more proactive talent experience

 

Use Case 4: Cross-Module HR Orchestration

One limitation of traditional business rules is that they often operate within a single application or module, using an employee relocation to illustrate this difference.

Traditional Automation: When an employee transfers from London to Singapore:

  • Update legal entity.
  • Change payroll information.
  • Modify benefits.
  • Update organizational assignment.

Agentic AI: The AI agent can also:

  • Creates a relocation checklist.
  • Schedules tax compliance activities.
  • Recommends local cultural training.
  • Notifies facilities.
  • Coordinates IT provisioning.
  • Updates travel profiles.
  • Informs the manager of pending actions.
  • Tracks completion across all systems.

Output: Instead of automating isolated transactions, the AI agent orchestrates the entire business process.

 

Use Case 5: Intelligent Exception Handling

Traditionally, business rules are designed for known scenarios, and they often struggle with exceptions. Consider a rule that rejects candidates under the legal hiring age.

Agentic AI: An AI agent can recognize contextual nuances such as internship programs, country-specific labor regulations, or parental consent requirements. Rather than rejecting the transaction outright, it can request additional documentation or escalate the case to HR for further review.

Output: AI enables recruiters and organisations to handle exceptions more effectively without compromising compliance.

 

Business Values in Effective Governance and System Administration

As AI agents become increasingly autonomous, governance becomes a critical success factor. Organizations should establish clear policies regarding:

  • Which decisions AI agents can make independently.
  • Which decisions require human approval.
  • How AI recommendations are audited.
  • How explanations are presented to users.
  • How role-based security and data privacy are enforced.

The model described above shows that business rules remain the authoritative source of organizational policy, while AI agents operate within those predefined boundaries. The future of SAP SuccessFactors is not about replacing business rules with AI. It is about combining deterministic automation with intelligent orchestration.

Business rules continue to answer: "Is this action allowed?"

Agentic AI answers: "How can I achieve the desired outcome while complying with those rules?"

Organizations that successfully combine these two capabilities will move beyond automating individual HR tasks to orchestrating complete employee journeys from hiring and onboarding to development, mobility, compensation, and retirement. That is where the real value of Agentic AI lies: not in replacing established business processes, but in making them more intelligent, adaptive, and outcome driven.
